target.title: Add snapshot SQLite stress harness
target.body:
Depends on #94805.RFC: openclaw/rfcs#20Related: #94675, #94646## Summary- Adds an opt-in SQLite snapshot stress harness for the core `snapshot` work.- Exercises snapshot create/copy/restore while a concurrent writer commits transaction batches into the same source SQLite database.- Covers both Phase 1 DB units: `--target global` and `--agent <id>`.- Keeps writer lifecycle monitoring attached for the full snapshot loop so worker result/error/exit states fail deterministically instead of being missed.- Supports repeated runs against the same `--state-dir` by continuing batch IDs and using run-scoped sync/restore scratch paths.- Adds package scripts for smoke/default/large profiles.## ScopeThis is measurement and validation plumbing only. It does not add WAL bundles, scheduling, object storage, failover orchestration, or CI-gated long stress runs.## Real behavior proofBehavior or issue addressed:Core snapshot artifacts need a repeatable stress harness that proves restored safe-sync files remain SQLite-valid and do not expose partial writer transactions while the source DB is being mutated. This update also addresses review findings by keeping a single writer monitor attached from start through stop and making documented `--state-dir` reuse repeatable.Real environment tested:Windows checkout at `giodl/snapshot-stress-pr5` (`50bc0b102e`), stacked on PR #94805 head `86f92a1e70`, Node v22.22.3, source harness using `node:sqlite`, OpenClaw local SQLite state databases, and the stacked core snapshot provider from this PR stack. Node prints the expected experimental warning for `node:sqlite`.Exact steps or command run after this patch:1. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts`2. `pnpm exec oxlint scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.ts test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts`3. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.ts scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.mjs test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts package.json`4. `git diff --check`5. Repeat-run proof: run `node --import tsx scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.mjs --profile smoke --state-dir <same-dir> --output <one.json>`, then run the same command again with `<two.json>`.6. `pnpm test:snapshot:stress`7. `pnpm test:snapshot:stress:large`8. `node --import tsx scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.mjs --profile default --agent main --output .artifacts/snapshot-stress/agent-default.json`9. `node --import tsx scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.mjs --profile large --agent main --output .artifacts/snapshot-stress/agent-large.json`Evidence after fix:Focused validation passed:```textTest Files  1 passed (1)Tests       5 passed (5)Duration    900ms```Repeated `--state-dir` smoke proof passed twice in the same directory:```textSNAPSHOT_STRESS_PROFILE=smokeSNAPSHOT_STRESS_TARGET=globalSNAPSHOT_STRESS_ITERATIONS=3SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORES_VERIFIED=3SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WRITER_ROWS=32SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_P95_MS=25.832SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORE_P95_MS=9.431SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_BYTES_MAX=962560SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WAL_BYTES_AFTER=0--- second ---SNAPSHOT_STRESS_PROFILE=smokeSNAPSHOT_STRESS_TARGET=globalSNAPSHOT_STRESS_ITERATIONS=3SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORES_VERIFIED=3SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WRITER_ROWS=32SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_P95_MS=25.595SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORE_P95_MS=9.664SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_BYTES_MAX=962560SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WAL_BYTES_AFTER=0```Global default proof:```textSNAPSHOT_STRESS_PROFILE=defaultSNAPSHOT_STRESS_TARGET=globalSNAPSHOT_STRESS_ITERATIONS=20SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORES_VERIFIED=20SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WRITER_ROWS=736SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_P95_MS=22.898SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORE_P95_MS=10.462SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_BYTES_MAX=995328SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WAL_BYTES_AFTER=0```Global large proof:```textSNAPSHOT_STRESS_PROFILE=largeSNAPSHOT_STRESS_TARGET=globalSNAPSHOT_STRESS_ITERATIONS=100SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORES_VERIFIED=100SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WRITER_ROWS=7648SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_P95_MS=22.478SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORE_P95_MS=14.069SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_BYTES_MAX=1290240SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WAL_BYTES_AFTER=0```Agent default proof:```textSNAPSHOT_STRESS_PROFILE=defaultSNAPSHOT_STRESS_TARGET=agent:mainSNAPSHOT_STRESS_ITERATIONS=20SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORES_VERIFIED=20SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WRITER_ROWS=528SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_P95_MS=16.522SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORE_P95_MS=6.868SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_BYTES_MAX=147456SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WAL_BYTES_AFTER=0```Agent large proof:```textSNAPSHOT_STRESS_PROFILE=largeSNAPSHOT_STRESS_TARGET=agent:mainSNAPSHOT_STRESS_ITERATIONS=100SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORES_VERIFIED=100SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WRITER_ROWS=5248SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_P95_MS=15.794SNAPSHOT_STRESS_RESTORE_P95_MS=8.328SNAPSHOT_STRESS_SNAPSHOT_BYTES_MAX=348160SNAPSHOT_STRESS_WAL_BYTES_AFTER=0```Observed result after fix:The harness created snapshots while a writer committed transaction batches, copied each snapshot to a simulated synced location, restored every copied artifact to a separate SQLite file, verified `PRAGMA integrity_check`, and rejected partial batch visibility. The current Windows default/large runs verified 240 restores across global and per-agent targets with 13,160 committed writer rows total. The writer lifecycle monitor now stays attached throughout the snapshot loop, and repeated `--state-dir` runs no longer collide on stress table batch IDs or sync/restore output paths.Supporting validation passed:- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts`- `pnpm exec oxlint scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.ts test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts`- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.ts scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.mjs test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts package.json`- `git diff --check`What was not tested:Cross-platform stress, object-storage sync, WAL bundle/delta behavior, and production failover orchestration are intentionally left for later validation. Local Crabbox/Testbox proof was not run because this Windows/WSL machine does not currently have the Crabbox wrapper binary available in the expected WSL locations.Post-rebase validation on 50bc0b102e:- ode scripts/run-vitest.mjs run test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts passed 1 file / 5 tests.- pnpm exec oxlint scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.ts test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts passed.- pnpm exec oxfmt --check scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.ts scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.mjs test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts package.json passed.- git diff --check giodl/snapshot-restore-proof-pr4...HEAD passed.- pnpm test:snapshot:stress:smoke passed on the rebased stack.
Post-rebase refresh on `50bc0b102e` over #94805 head `86f92a1e70`:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts` passed 1 file / 5 tests
- `pnpm exec oxlint scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.ts test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts` passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.ts scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.mjs test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts package.json` passed
- `git diff --check giodl/snapshot-restore-proof-pr4...HEAD` passed
- `pnpm test:snapshot:stress:smoke` passed with 3 snapshots/restores verified, 32 writer rows, snapshot p95 25.814 ms, restore p95 9.590 ms, max artifact bytes 962560, WAL bytes after 0
- `codex review --base origin/giodl/snapshot-restore-proof-pr4` returned no discrete correctness issue

target.title: Add reset family carryover summaries
target.body:
## Behavior or issue addressed

Depends on #90239.

When a session has been reset/rotated, the active transcript can be clean while the previous reset archive still contains the latest compaction summary. The next embedded-agent prompt therefore starts without the carryover context unless the model/tool explicitly asks for family history.

This change reuses the session-family transcript resolver from #90239 and injects the latest reset-archive compaction summary into embedded-agent prompt context before the LLM boundary, but only when the active context does not already contain a compaction summary.

## Fix

- Move the #90239 family/reset transcript target resolver into a shared gateway helper.
- Keep `chat.history(includeFamily)` on the shared resolver.
- Pass the current session store path/entry into embedded-agent attempts.
- Add a prompt-context transform that prepends one `compactionSummary` from reset ancestor transcripts.
- Skip injection when the active session already has a compaction summary.
- Cap family transcript read targets before reading files; ancestor targets stop at the cap while current-session targets remain available.
- Rebased onto current `origin/main` and preserved the newer transcript-reader seam plus same-session reset fallback.

## Real behavior proof

**Current head proven:** `90525a0927bfcd94912dfa9c3d702c6de6cb231d`.

**Behavior or issue addressed:** A reset/rotated logical session should read bounded reset-family history through the shared resolver, keep current reset archive plus current active transcript available inside the cap, inject exactly one reset-family compaction summary before the embedded-agent LLM boundary, and cap oversized archived summaries before prompt injection.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on this PR branch at `/home/clever/.openclaw/workspace/github/openclaw`, using current branch TypeScript modules and temporary on-disk session stores/transcripts under `/tmp/openclaw-family-proof-905-*`. No private channel, endpoint, phone, prompt, or auth data was used.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```text
$ git rev-parse HEAD
90525a0927bfcd94912dfa9c3d702c6de6cb231d

$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'TS'
# The script imported these current-branch modules:
# - ./src/gateway/session-history-family.ts
# - ./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/session-family-carryover.ts
# It created a temporary sessions.json, 35 ancestor transcripts,
# one current reset archive transcript, and one current active transcript.
# Ancestor 29 contained an oversized compaction summary larger than
# CARRYOVER_SUMMARY_MAX_CHARS. The script called the real family target
# resolver, real carryover resolver, installed the prompt transform,
# applied the transform twice, printed the observed result, and removed temp files.
TS
{
  "head": "90525a0927bfcd94912dfa9c3d702c6de6cb231d",
  "targetCount": 32,
  "firstTargets": [
    "ancestor-proof-00:ancestor",
    "ancestor-proof-01:ancestor",
    "ancestor-proof-02:ancestor"
  ],
  "lastTargets": [
    "ancestor-proof-29:ancestor",
    "current-proof-session:reset-archive",
    "current-proof-session:active"
  ],
  "currentResetTargetPresent": true,
  "currentActiveLast": true,
  "ancestor29Present": true,
  "ancestor30Present": false,
  "ancestor34Present": false,
  "carryoverSummaryLength": 16384,
  "carryoverSummaryMaxChars": 16384,
  "carryoverSummaryEndsTruncated": true,
  "carryoverSummaryPrefix": "Exact-head 905 oversized carryover 29: x",
  "carryoverTokensBefore": 2029,
  "promptRolesBeforeLlm": [
    "compactionSummary",
    "user"
  ],
  "compactionSummaryCount": 1,
  "idempotentRoles": [
    "compactionSummary",
    "user"
  ]
}
```

**Evidence after fix:**

- The shared resolver returned 32 targets and kept both current reset archive and current active transcript inside the cap.
- The current active transcript was ordered last for final history trimming.
- Ancestors beyond the reserved cap were not read: `ancestor30Present` and `ancestor34Present` are false.
- Carryover selected the latest ancestor inside the capped family window, `ancestor-proof-29`, and preserved its token count.
- The oversized archived compaction summary was capped before prompt injection: `carryoverSummaryLength` equals `carryoverSummaryMaxChars` (16384) and `carryoverSummaryEndsTruncated` is true.
- The embedded prompt transform injected exactly one `compactionSummary` before the model boundary and remained idempotent on a second pass.

**Observed result after fix:** The terminal output showed `targetCount: 32`, current reset archive plus current active transcript retained at the end of the target list, `ancestor30Present: false`, `ancestor34Present: false`, one pre-LLM `compactionSummary`, idempotent second transform roles, and an oversized summary capped exactly at 16384 chars with `[summary truncated]`.

**Validation on current head:**

```text
$ CI=true node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-models.test.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  20 passed (20)

$ CI=true node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/session-family-carryover.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  8 passed (8)

$ git diff --check
# no output

$ CI=true node --import tsx scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check
Prompt snapshots are current (7 files).

$ CI=true node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json
# passed
```

target.title: Add core SQLite snapshot command
target.body:
## SummaryThis PR adds a core SQLite snapshot command for OpenClaw-owned state.It includes:- shared SQLite snapshot provider and local artifact repository- `openclaw snapshot create|verify|restore|list`- named OpenClaw database targets: `--target global` and `--agent <id>`- explicit `--db <path>` support for advanced/manual SQLite files- realpath resolution for named targets and explicit `--db` paths before snapshotting- global-state artifact sanitization for transient `delivery_queue_entries`, matching the backup recovery invariant- restore cleanup for stale `-wal`, `-shm`, and `-journal` sidecars- docs for which files are safe to sync, which live SQLite sidecars should be ignored, and why snapshot artifacts must still be protected as sensitive state- docs that distinguish `openclaw snapshot` from `openclaw backup``openclaw snapshot` is the narrow per-database SQLite sync artifact. `openclaw backup` remains the broader `.tar.gz` recovery archive for state, config, credentials, sessions, and optional workspaces.## WhyThe goal from #94675 is not to make hosts copy live SQLite files more carefully. The goal is for OpenClaw to produce a SQLite-safe sync artifact.This PR establishes that boundary:- live `.sqlite`, `-wal`, `-shm`, and `-journal` files remain hot local runtime state- `openclaw snapshot create` materializes a portable artifact directory containing `manifest.json` and `database.sqlite`- hosts, containers, object-store sync jobs, and backup systems should sync the completed snapshot directory, not the live SQLite file family- restore hydrates a fresh local SQLite file from the verified artifact before OpenClaw opens itThe Lobster-shaped host case is covered by this contract: hosts ask OpenClaw for named database targets and sync completed artifacts. This PR intentionally does not add a `memory-search` target, because memory-search currently shares the per-agent database and a memory-only export needs a separate design.## StackThis PR combines the previous core implementation slices:- former #94694: core snapshot provider proof- former #94717: core `openclaw snapshot` CLI- former #94799: named OpenClaw database targets- this PR: safe-sync artifact, restore proof/docs, realpath handling, and recovery invariantsFollow-up validation PR: #94967RFC: openclaw/rfcs#20Related: #94675, #94646## ScopeThis does not add memory-search-only export, WAL bundle deltas, object storage upload, scheduling, leases, failover automation, restore-on-boot behavior, production orchestration, or a new memory-search path config. Those remain future phases or separate maintainer decisions. This PR ships the local core command and artifact contract.## Real behavior proofBehavior or issue addressed:OpenClaw can convert OpenClaw-owned SQLite state into syncable artifact directories for global and per-agent databases, verify them, copy them independently from live SQLite files, and restore them into verified fresh SQLite databases. Global snapshot artifacts also scrub transient delivery queue rows, and restore removes stale SQLite sidecars before opening the restored database.Real environment tested:Windows checkout at `C:\src\openclaw-snapshot-pr1`, PR head `86f92a1e70`, Node v22.22.3, `pnpm build` packaged output, `node openclaw.mjs` launcher commands, temporary local SQLite databases created with Node `node:sqlite`, local snapshot repository under `C:\tmp\openclaw-snapshot-final-proof`, plus source Vitest harnesses with WAL-mode SQLite databases, named OpenClaw database targets, realpath coverage, global transient queue scrubbing, rollback journal cleanup, and config schema validation.Exact steps or command run after this patch:1. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/snapshot.test.ts src/snapshot/snapshot-provider.test.ts src/cli/program/register.snapshot.test.ts src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`2. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/cli/snapshot.md src/commands/snapshot.ts src/commands/snapshot.test.ts src/snapshot/local-repository.ts src/snapshot/snapshot-provider.ts src/snapshot/snapshot-provider.test.ts src/cli/program/register.snapshot.ts src/cli/program/register.snapshot.test.ts src/agents/memory-search.ts src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/types.tools.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`3. `git diff --check`4. `pnpm build`5. `codex review --base origin/main`6. Packaged `node openclaw.mjs snapshot create|verify|restore` proof for canonical global and per-agent databases from the built launcher.Evidence after fix:Focused validation passed 5 Vitest shards:- `src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`: 35 tests- `src/snapshot/snapshot-provider.test.ts`: 6 tests- `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts`: 34 tests- `src/cli/program/register.snapshot.test.ts`: 7 tests- `src/commands/snapshot.test.ts`: 9 tests`pnpm build` passed at `86f92a1e70`.`codex review --base origin/main` returned:```textNo actionable regressions were found in the snapshot command, provider, manifest validation, or SQLite snapshot helper paths. Focused snapshot CLI/provider/registration tests passed.```Packaged CLI proof from global create returned `ok: true`, `database.id: "global"`, `database.kind: "global-control-plane"`, `database.basename: "openclaw.sqlite"`, `database.userVersion: 410`, artifact path `database.sqlite`, artifact size `8192`, and a `sha256:<64 hex chars>` artifact hash.Packaged CLI proof from per-agent create returned `ok: true`, `database.id: "agent:main"`, `database.kind: "agent-data-plane"`, `database.basename: "openclaw-agent.sqlite"`, `database.userVersion: 411`, artifact path `database.sqlite`, artifact size `8192`, and a `sha256:<64 hex chars>` artifact hash.Packaged CLI output from verify and restore returned `ok: true` and `integrityCheck: ["ok"]` for both snapshot paths.Readback from the restored SQLite DBs returned:```json{  "global": {    "rows": [{ "value": "global-proof" }],    "userVersion": { "user_version": 410 }  },  "agent": {    "rows": [{ "value": "agent-proof" }],    "userVersion": { "user_version": 411 }  }}```Observed result after fix:The packaged CLI created verified snapshot artifacts from the canonical global and per-agent databases, verified both artifacts, restored both to fresh SQLite files, preserved `PRAGMA user_version`, and preserved the expected rows. Unit coverage also proves named targets and explicit `--db` sources resolve through `realpath` before snapshotting, global artifacts scrub transient delivery queue rows without mutating the live source DB, and restore removes stale WAL/SHM/rollback-journal sidecars.Docs proof after ClawSweeper P1: `docs/cli/snapshot.md` now warns that global and per-agent snapshot artifacts can include auth profile records, session state, plugin state, and credentials-adjacent data, and that snapshot repositories need the same access controls/encryption/retention restrictions as backups and live state. It also documents restore cleanup for stale `-journal` sidecars. `pnpm docs:check-mdx`, `pnpm docs:check-links`, `pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/cli/snapshot.md`, and `git diff --check` passed at `86f92a1e70`.

What was not tested:No live gateway runtime, Azure Blob upload, Lobster adapter integration, scheduled backup loop, restore-on-boot path, lease/failover path, cross-platform stress, memory-search-only export, or WAL bundle/delta behavior was tested in this PR. Stress validation is tracked separately in #94967.

target.title: feat(plugins): add Control UI entry points
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Plugins can expose gateway-authenticated HTTP routes, but there is no generic way for a plugin to register a first-class Control UI navigation entry.
- Why it matters: Plugin UIs currently require bespoke Control UI wiring, which makes real plugin UI surfaces harder to build, test, and keep out of core.
- What changed: Added generic Plugin UI Entry Points across the plugin registry, gateway protocol, launch-token plumbing, and Control UI navigation/rendering.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add the Session Search plugin or any plugin-specific UI. A separate proof plugin lives on [`Ittiz/openclaw@forge/session-search-plugin`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/tree/forge/session-search-plugin).

## Working Example Plugin

The working example is the Session Search plugin on the plugin-only proof branch:

- Branch: [`Ittiz/openclaw@forge/session-search-plugin`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/tree/forge/session-search-plugin)
- README: [`README.md`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/blob/forge/session-search-plugin/README.md)
- Entry point registration: [`index.ts`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/blob/forge/session-search-plugin/index.ts)
- Manifest: [`openclaw.plugin.json`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/blob/forge/session-search-plugin/openclaw.plugin.json)

That branch intentionally contains only the plugin files, not the OpenClaw source tree. It demonstrates a real app-nav Control UI plugin that searches, inspects, injects, and resumes prior sessions through the entry-point API added by this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Current-head dispatch/proof refresh: `4e36e35e2205382b8ee73df4ed0ef80bf53435e8` fixes the ClawSweeper finding that `plugins.uiEntryPoints` and `plugins.uiEntryPointLaunch` were defined but not registered in `coreGatewayHandlers`.
- Exact commands run after the dispatch fix:

```bash
git diff --check
corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server-methods.ts src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-host-hooks.test.ts src/agents/tool-display-config.ts
node --import tsx scripts/tool-display.ts --check
corepack pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-host-hooks.test.ts
corepack pnpm test src/gateway/method-scopes.test.ts
corepack pnpm build
```

- Current-head evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-host-hooks.test.ts
```

- Observed result after the dispatch fix: The new regression test invokes `plugins.uiEntryPoints` and `plugins.uiEntryPointLaunch` through `coreGatewayHandlers`, proving the real gateway dispatch map now reaches the plugin-host hook handlers instead of falling through to unknown-method handling. The build gate also passes on the fixed head.

- Behavior or issue addressed: A plugin can register a Control UI app-nav entry, have it listed by the gateway, launch it through a short-lived token, and render it in Control UI without hardcoded plugin UI code. Current head tested: `87e108f70eb4eb2b7b5c540b9a574bebe0845c84`.
- Real environment tested: Local Linux checkout in the PR worktree after merging latest upstream main, plus the running OpenClaw development gateway on `127.0.0.1:18789` with the real Session Search plugin registered as a Control UI app-nav entry. Browser proof used Playwright against system Chromium and the live Control UI; gateway token was used locally and redacted from output.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:



- Observed result after fix: The Control UI app navigation displayed the real Session Search plugin entry, clicking it launched a sandboxed iframe through `plugins.uiEntryPointLaunch`, the iframe rendered the plugin title/description inside Control UI, and unauthenticated direct route access stayed blocked with `401 Unauthorized`. The branch is also merged with latest upstream main, the missing UI spawn helper exports are restored while keeping Corepack fallback behavior, the host-hook fixture registers the new entry-point seam, and generated i18n metadata is clean.
- What was not tested: I did not rerun the full hosted GitHub matrix locally; GitHub CI is responsible for the complete shard set. The live browser proof used an existing development gateway and plugin instance rather than starting a separate throwaway gateway.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A.

- Root cause: N/A.
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-host-hooks.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.plugin-http-auth.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/navigation.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/navigation.browser.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Plugin UI entry-point registration, gateway listing, launch-token validation, plugin HTTP auth behavior, and Control UI navigation.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests cover the plugin API, gateway auth/protocol seam, and Control UI navigation without depending on one specific plugin implementation.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests are included.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Plugins can now add authenticated Control UI navigation entries through the plugin API. No default UI changes are visible unless a plugin registers an entry point.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[plugin registers route] -> [no generic Control UI nav entry]

After:
[plugin registers entry point]
  -> [gateway lists entry point]
  -> [Control UI renders navigation item]
  -> [launch token opens authenticated plugin UI route]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Plugins can expose Control UI entry points, so entry points are scoped and served through gateway-authenticated plugin routes.
  - Launch uses short-lived plugin UI entry tokens instead of unauthenticated direct access.
  - Entry points declare required operator scopes.
  - Tests cover unauthorized access rejection and launch-token validation.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Local source checkout, Node.js, pnpm/corepack
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI + gateway plugin host
- Relevant config (redacted): Development gateway with a real plugin registering a Control UI entry point

### Steps

1. Apply this branch.
2. Register a plugin with `registerControlUiEntryPoint`.
3. Start the gateway from the rebuilt checkout.
4. Open Control UI.
5. Confirm the plugin entry appears in app navigation.
6. Open the plugin entry.
7. Attempt direct unauthenticated access to the plugin route.

### Expected

- Registered plugin UI entry appears in Control UI navigation.
- Opening the entry launches the plugin UI through authenticated gateway plumbing.
- Direct unauthenticated route access is rejected.

### Actual

- Entry rendered in Control UI.
- Plugin UI opened through the Control UI entry.
- Direct unauthenticated plugin route returned `401`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Browser diagnostic output
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Generic plugin entry-point registration.
  - Gateway method/listing behavior.
  - Plugin route auth behavior.
  - Control UI navigation rendering.
  - Real plugin proof using [`Ittiz/openclaw@forge/session-search-plugin`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/tree/forge/session-search-plugin).
- Edge cases checked:
  - Missing/invalid launch token is rejected.
  - Plugin-specific names are not hardcoded into core entry-point plumbing.
  - Direct unauthenticated plugin route access remains blocked.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Packaged release install from npm/tarball.
  - Mobile/native Control UI surfaces.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Plugins could expose confusing or unauthorized UI surfaces.
  - Mitigation: Entry points declare required scopes and launch through gateway-authenticated token plumbing.

- Risk: Core could accidentally become tailored to one proof plugin.
  - Mitigation: Core tests use neutral fixture plugin names; the real Session Search proof plugin is kept separate on [`Ittiz/openclaw@forge/session-search-plugin`](https://github.com/Ittiz/openclaw/tree/forge/session-search-plugin).

target.title: fix(agent-core): skip unreadable tool names
target.body:
## Summary

- guard tool-name lookup in agent-core tool execution and llm-core validation so unreadable sibling tool names do not abort healthy tool calls
- preserve the per-tool sequential safety contract by treating unreadable `executionMode` on a matched tool as sequential
- add regression coverage for healthy tool execution, llm-core validation, and the sequential fallback path

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/agent-core/src/agent-loop.test.ts packages/llm-core/src/validation.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint packages/agent-core/src/agent-loop.ts packages/agent-core/src/agent-loop.test.ts packages/llm-core/src/validation.ts packages/llm-core/src/validation.test.ts`
- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`
- direct `node --import tsx` repro: unreadable sibling `name` getter no longer blocks `healthy_probe`; observed tool result text `ok`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: model-emitted tool calls can execute the requested healthy tool even when another registered tool has an unreadable `name` accessor; unreadable `executionMode` on the matched tool fails closed to sequential execution.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw worktree on Node/Vitest via repo wrappers; remote changed-gate proof attempted via Testbox and AWS Crabbox.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/agent-core/src/agent-loop.test.ts packages/llm-core/src/validation.test.ts --reporter=dot`; direct `node --import tsx` repro invoking `runAgentLoop`; `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"`; retry with `--debug`.

Evidence after fix: focused tests passed, 2 files / 7 tests; direct repro printed `ok`; oxlint and diff check passed; autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings.

Observed result after fix: the healthy requested tool returns a non-error `toolResult`; the sequential fallback regression test proves the guarded tool finishes before its sibling starts.

What was not tested: `pnpm check:changed` did not complete remotely. Blacksmith Testbox was not authenticated and the non-interactive auth path opened an interactive browser flow. AWS Crabbox first failed before usable proof with coordinator HTTP 500 (`Durable Object's isolate exceeded its memory limit and was reset`), then the debug retry failed with HTTP 429 `cost_limit_exceeded` / active lease limit exceeded.

target.title: fix(agents): harden bundle MCP catalog projection
target.body:
## Summary

- Materialize bundle MCP catalog server/tool descriptors through guarded field reads before sorting, projection, and execution closure creation.
- Keep healthy sibling tools and utility tools when a malformed catalog entry or server metadata accessor throws; surface diagnostics instead of crashing.
- Thread projection diagnostics through runtime materialization and CLI MCP probe output.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Bundle MCP catalog descriptors with throwing top-level accessors no longer crash tool projection or request materialization; unreadable required fields are skipped with diagnostics, and optional descriptions fall back.

Real environment tested: local macOS Codex worktree; Azure Crabbox Linux runner `cbx_c84bdac5263f`, run `run_3263f0edb22a`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

target.title: fix(discord): disable ws 8.21.0 receiver part limits for gateway sockets
target.body:
## Summary

ws 8.21.0 introduced `maxBufferedChunks` and `maxFragments` receiver limits (commit `[security] Limit retained message parts`) that can trigger `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and close connections with code 1008 under certain traffic patterns.

This PR explicitly sets both limits to `0` (disabled) for Discord gateway WebSocket connections, restoring the ws 8.20.0 behavior for the trusted Discord gateway peer while preserving the security fix for all other WebSocket usage.

## Changes

- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.ts`**: Base class `createWebSocket()` now passes `maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0` with a `ws.ClientOptions` cast (since `@types/ws` 8.18.1 does not yet expose these fields).
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.ts`**: Extended `DiscordGatewayWebSocketCtor` type to include the new options; monitor gateway creation path passes the same disabled limits.
- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.test.ts`**: Added test verifying base class WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.test.ts`**: Added test verifying monitor plugin WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.proxy.test.ts`**: Updated two existing exact-match assertions to include the new options.

## Why `0` instead of raising the limits

- ws 8.21.0 treats `0` as disabled (`receiver.js:61`, `receiver.js:98`: `if (this._maxBufferedChunks > 0 && ...)`), matching the pre-8.21.0 behavior exactly.
- Discord gateway is a trusted peer; the security limits are designed to protect against untrusted senders.
- Choosing an arbitrary higher threshold would be fragile without real production traffic data to calibrate against.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: ws 8.21.0's retained-part receiver limits (`maxFragments` default 131072 / `maxBufferedChunks` default 1048576) abort a Discord gateway socket with `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` / close `1008`; this PR sets both to `0` (disabled) on the Discord gateway sockets, restoring ws 8.20.0 behavior for that trusted peer.
- **Real environment tested**: Live Discord gateway connection from a Docker `node:22-bookworm-slim` container (Node `v22.22.3`) with the repo-pinned `ws@8.21.0` installed exactly, using a real bot token (redacted), connecting to `wss://gateway.discord.gg/?v=10&encoding=json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: build a `node:22-bookworm-slim` image with `npm install ws@8.21.0`, then run a client that constructs the gateway socket with this patch's options (`new ws.WebSocket(url, { handshakeTimeout: 30000, maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0 })`), completes the HELLO/IDENTIFY/READY handshake against the real Discord gateway, sends heartbeats, holds a 60s stable window, then closes with code 1000.
- **Evidence after fix**: Live Discord gateway run with this patch's options applied (token, `session_id`, and `resume_gateway_url` redacted):

```
[env] node=v22.22.3 ws=8.21.0
[discord] connecting wss://gateway.discord.gg (maxBufferedChunks:0, maxFragments:0)
[discord] open
[discord] HELLO heartbeat_interval=41250ms
[discord] IDENTIFY sent (token redacted)
[discord] READY received (session_id/resume_gateway_url redacted)
[discord] HEARTBEAT_ACK #1
[discord] stable window 60000ms complete
[discord] close code=1000 reason=<empty>
[result] PASS live: READY + 1 heartbeat ACK; no WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS during stable window
```

Supplemental deterministic mechanism repro against a local ws 8.21.0 gateway streaming one message in 131073 fragments (one over the client default `maxFragments`), exercising the same `ws.WebSocket` receiver path:

```
# default ws.WebSocket (ws 8.21 defaults)
[before] open
[before] error code=WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS message=Too many message fragments
[before] close code=1006 reason=<empty>

# this patch's options applied
[after] open
[after] message received: 131073 bytes - NO receiver-limit close
```

- **Observed result after fix**: On the real Discord gateway, with the disabled limits applied, the connection reaches `READY`, exchanges heartbeats, and stays connected through the 60s window with no `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and no `1008` close; the only close is the intentional client-initiated `1000`. The deterministic repro confirms the same constructor options remove the receiver-limit close that the default ws 8.21 client hits.
- **What was not tested**: the live run confirms a real gateway session stays healthy with the patched options, but does not force Discord's production fragmentation pattern on demand, does not exercise the `zlib-stream` transport, and does not drive the full OpenClaw plugin wiring end-to-end. The deterministic repro proves the ws 8.21 retained-part mechanism and that this patch's options disable the exact receiver checks, but uses a local peer rather than Discord's live framing.

## Tests

- `gateway.test.ts`: 25 passed
- `gateway-plugin.test.ts`: 17 passed  
- `provider.proxy.test.ts`: 21 passed
- TypeScript check (tsgo): clean

Fixes #88330

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`**
- Add `yieldBetweenHandlers` option with `setImmediate` yield between hook handlers
- Add per-handler timing instrumentation with handler index reporting
- Log warnings for slow handlers exceeding 500ms threshold

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable `yieldBetweenHandlers` for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Support new async workspace file loading and timing callback interfaces

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — 3 new tests for yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning

All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

target.title: fix: skip setup-only channel plugins in outbound resolution and drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect
target.body:
## Summary

When the qqbot WebSocket reconnects after a session timeout (close code `4009`), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returns a setup-only plugin stub that lacks any send capability. This causes the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error, and all messages queued during the disconnection window are permanently lost.

## Root Cause

`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolves the first matching plugin entry for a channel id without verifying that the entry actually has send capability (`outbound.sendText`, `message.send.text`, etc.). During qqbot WebSocket reconnection, a setup-only plugin entry (registered for configuration/pairing but not for message delivery) shadows the real sending plugin, causing the resolution to short-circuit with a non-functional stub.

Regression introduced in 2026.5.28 (commit `e932160`).

## Fix

### 1. Split outbound channel resolution into shared and delivery paths (`channel-resolution.ts`)

Add a `channelPluginCanSend()` predicate that checks for any of the 10 known send methods across both the legacy `outbound` adapter and the newer `message.send` adapter:

- `outbound.sendText / sendMedia / sendPayload / sendPoll / sendFormattedText / sendFormattedMedia`
- `message.send.text / media / payload / poll`

Instead of gating the shared `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolver (which would break action-only channel plugins that have `actions.handleAction` but no send capability), a new **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`** resolver applies the send-capability guard exclusively for message delivery paths.

- **`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()`**: Shared resolver — returns any matching plugin regardless of send capability, preserving resolution for action dispatch, target resolution, and other non-send callers.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`**: Delivery resolver — skips setup-only entries and only returns plugins that can actually send messages.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelMessageAdapter()`**: Updated to use the delivery resolver.
- **`message.ts`**: `sendMessage`/`sendPoll` updated to use the delivery resolver.

### 2. Drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect (`extensions/qqbot/src/channel.ts`)

The `onReady` and `onResumed` gateway callbacks now call `drainPendingDeliveries()` from the plugin SDK delivery queue runtime, recovering messages that were queued while the WebSocket was disconnected. The drain is:

- Filtered by channel (`qqbot`) and account id, with a fallback for default-account entries where `accountId` may be `undefined`
- Deduplicated via `drainKey` to prevent concurrent drains
- Wrapped in try/catch so drain failures do not break the reconnection flow

## Tests

- `channel-resolution.test.ts`: 17 tests (5 new — setup-only plugin returns from shared resolver, action-only plugin resolves from shared but not delivery resolver, delivery resolver skips setup-only and returns sending fallback, legacy outbound adapter, poll-only outbound)
- `channel.test.ts`: 2 tests (new file — onReady drain, onResumed drain)

Fixes #88955.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After a QQ Bot WebSocket disconnect (session takeover / close code 4009), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returned a setup-only plugin stub instead of the runtime entry, causing `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` and dropping all pending deliveries.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw `2026.6.2` (commit `b90683e`, branch `fix/qqbot-reconnect-outbound-88955`), macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. QQ Bot plugin `@openclaw/qqbot@2026.5.31-beta.4` with live QQ Bot credentials (appId `<redacted>`).

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Built patched OpenClaw from fix branch: `pnpm build && npm pack --ignore-scripts`
  2. Installed globally: `npm install -g openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz` — confirmed version: `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (b90683e)`
  3. Restarted gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  4. Observed qqbot plugin startup, WebSocket connection, and `Gateway ready` callback sequence in logs
  5. Verified normal QQ Bot C2C message round-trip: user sent "hello" → bot received → processed → replied with markdown (20 chars, 200 OK)
  6. Scanned full gateway log for `"Outbound not configured"` errors — zero matches
  7. Repeated restart cycle to confirm reconnect stability across multiple `Gateway ready` events

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  # Patched OpenClaw installed and running
  $ openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (b90683e)

  # Gateway restart sequence — qqbot reconnects cleanly
  2026-06-02T22:38:53.727+08:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; restarting
  2026-06-02T22:39:15.986+08:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: ..., qqbot, ...; 1.6s)
  2026-06-02T22:39:16.171+08:00 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Starting gateway — appId=<redacted>, enabled=true
  2026-06-02T22:39:17.387+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Connecting to wss://api.sgroup.qq.com/websocket
  2026-06-02T22:39:18.468+08:00 [qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
  2026-06-02T22:39:18.641+08:00 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway ready

  # Successful QQ Bot message delivery after reconnect
  2026-06-02T22:32:40.229+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Processing message from <redacted>: hello
  2026-06-02T22:32:59.431+08:00 [qqbot] [default] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 200 OK
  2026-06-02T22:32:59.432+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Sent markdown chunk (20/20 chars) with 0 HTTP images (c2c)

  # Zero outbound resolution errors across entire log history
  $ grep -c "Outbound not configured.*qqbot" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log
  0

  # Multiple Gateway ready events over 4+ hours — all clean reconnects
  $ grep "qqbot.*Gateway ready" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log | wc -l
  18
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: The delivery resolver correctly skipped the setup-only plugin entry (which has no `sendText`/`sendMedia` methods) and resolved the runtime entry with full send capability. Outbound messages delivered successfully through the QQ Bot API (HTTP 200). Zero occurrences of the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error across 18 reconnect cycles over a 4+ hour observation window. The shared resolver continued to return all plugin entries for non-send callers (action dispatch, target resolution), preserving action-only plugin compatibility.

- **What was not tested**:

  Live drain proof is not reproducible on the fixed build. The `drainReconnectPendingDeliveries()` mechanism (Fix 2) was verified via unit tests but not observed draining actual queued entries in a live environment. This is not an oversight — it is a structural consequence of the resolver fix (Fix 1) eliminating the only deterministic cause of pending qqbot entries. Code-level analysis confirms this:

  **How the delivery queue works** (`deliver.ts:1254-1280`): Every outbound delivery is write-ahead enqueued via `enqueueDelivery()` before any send attempt. On success, `ackDelivery()` removes the entry (`deliver.ts:1367`). On throw, `failDelivery()` bumps `retryCount` and **leaves it pending** (`delivery-queue-storage.ts:137-144`).

  **What created the pending entries in #88955**: The `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` throw fired from `createChannelHandler` (`deliver.ts:223-231`) when `resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery` returned a setup-only plugin with no send methods. This throw occurs **outside** the per-payload try/catch (`deliver.ts:1451`), so it propagated to the queue-cleanup catch → `failDelivery` → entry left pending. The resolver fix eliminates this throw by filtering setup-only entries via `channelPluginCanSend()`.

  **Why transient QQ API failures also do not create pending entries**: The qqbot engine **swallows** HTTP/network errors into returned `{error}` result objects instead of throwing (`outbound.ts:301-304, 313-318`). The delivery core text branch records "sent" purely on `deliveredResults.length > 0` with no message-id identity gate (`deliver.ts:1772-1778`), so a swallowed engine error yields a "sent" outcome → `ackDelivery` → entry removed, not pending.

  **The drain's real value**: It is a belt-and-suspenders safeguard for the **upgrade path** — entries left pending (with `lastError: "Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`) by an unfixed build are drained immediately after a fixed gateway reconnects. The `bypassBackoff` predicate explicitly matches this exact error string (`/outbound not configured/i`), ensuring these entries skip retry backoff and are retried on the first reconnect event. If the retry still fails (e.g., the plugin is genuinely misconfigured), `isPermanentDeliveryError` (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:79`) moves the entry to `failed/` — no infinite retry loop. `MAX_RETRIES` is also enforced before the bypass branch (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:544`). The drain also covers the narrow crash-in-enqueue-to-ack window handled by `recoverPendingDeliveries` at startup (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:604`). The drain selection filter (`channel === "qqbot"` + account id match + bypassBackoff for `"Outbound not configured"`, `channel.test.ts:90-152`) and the reconnect-drain integration logic (`delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts`) are exercised by 35 unit tests (all passing).

  **Seeded pending-drain proof**: To validate the drain recovery path end-to-end, a pending delivery entry was manually seeded into the SQLite delivery queue to simulate the upgrade scenario (entries left by an unfixed build):

  1. Inserted a pending entry: `id=seeded-drain-test-001`, `channel=qqbot`, `accountId=default`, `lastError="Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`, `retry_count=1`
  2. Restarted gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Observed recovery sequence in logs:

  ```
  00:30:00.353 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: ..., qqbot, ...; 1.9s)
  00:30:00.987 [delivery-recovery] Found 1 pending delivery entries — starting recovery
  00:30:01.567 [qqbot] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 200 OK
  00:30:02.703 [delivery-recovery] Recovered delivery seeded-drain-test-001 on qqbot
  00:30:02.703 [delivery-recovery] Delivery recovery complete: 1 recovered, 0 failed, 0 skipped (max retries), 0 deferred (backoff)
  00:30:02.816 [qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
  00:30:02.952 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway ready
  ```

  4. Verified entry removed from DB: `SELECT count(*) FROM delivery_queue_entries WHERE channel='qqbot' AND status='pending'` → **0**
  5. Zero `"Outbound not configured"` errors after restart

  **Key observation**: The `0 deferred (backoff)` in the recovery summary confirms the `bypassBackoff` predicate (`/outbound not configured/i`) correctly matched the seeded entry's `lastError`, allowing immediate retry without waiting for backoff. The entry was successfully delivered (HTTP 200) and acked (removed from queue).

  **Note**: The startup `recoverPendingDeliveries` path (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:604`) executed before the qqbot `onReady` callback (`00:30:00.987` vs `00:30:02.952`), so startup recovery handled the entry before the reconnect drain fired. Both paths share the same underlying drain logic and bypass predicates; the startup path simply runs first. A naturally-occurring live drain on the `onReady` path cannot be reliably reproduced because the fix removes the deterministic failure that created those entries, and startup recovery pre-empts the reconnect drain for entries present at boot. Multi-shard configurations were not tested.

commands.review_summary:
The PR changes `web_fetch` so title-only Readability output falls through to provider fallback and then body-scoped raw HTML extraction, with tests for fake body tags in comments, raw-text elements, and templates.

PR surface: Source +212, Tests +233. Total +445 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the title-only Readability shape: current-main source accepts any non-empty Readability text before fallback, and the PR body supplies copied live output showing a forced title-only result falling through to `raw-html`. The exact reporter page is still unavailable.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] Maintainer review should decide whether the provider-first fallback compatibility and cost tradeoff is acceptable; there is no narrow mechanical blocker for a ClawSweeper repair job.

target.title: fix #82685: [Bug]: web_fetch fails to extract full HTML body content
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #82685 by treating title-only Readability output as failed body extraction instead of returning only the page title.
- Preserves the configured-provider-before-raw-HTML order for title-only Readability pages, so existing provider routing remains the first recovery path when configured.
- Hardens the runtime body scanner so fake `<body>` tags inside comments, raw-text containers, and inert/nested `<template>` regions do not replace the real visible document body.
- Keeps the change scoped to the `web_fetch` runtime extraction path; shared HTML-to-markdown helpers, provider configuration, network policy, and public API shape are unchanged.

What problem does this PR solve?

`web_fetch` could accept Readability output that only contained the page title as successful body extraction. In that state, users and agents could receive only the title instead of visible page content. The runtime body scanner also needed to preserve the document-body boundary while skipping HTML regions that can legally contain literal fake body markup but are not the visible body.

Why does this matter now?

The linked issue is user-visible: downstream tool output is misleading when `web_fetch` returns title-only text or hidden/inert fake body content instead of the actual page body.

What is the intended outcome?

This is a root-cause extraction-state fix: title-only Readability output no longer completes extraction, and body-boundary scanning now enforces the browser-visible body invariant before raw HTML text is produced. Successful behavior is provider output when a configured provider handles the title-only page, or visible body text from local raw HTML cleanup when no provider output is available.

What is intentionally out of scope?

This PR does not change shared `htmlToMarkdown`, provider configuration, network/auth/security policy, migrations, schema, public API shape, or the provider catalog. It only changes the `web_fetch` runtime extraction decision and focused regression coverage for that path.

What should reviewers focus on?

Please review the source-of-truth boundary between local Readability extraction, configured provider routing, and runtime-local raw HTML body cleanup. The provider compatibility boundary is preserved: title-only pages still try configured provider routing before local raw HTML cleanup, while scanner hardening only affects the local cleanup path when it is reached.

## Linked context

- Closes #82685.
- Updates PR #89028 after review feedback called out missing real-behavior proof, raw-text/template scanner gaps, and provider compatibility risk.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `web_fetch` no longer returns only the page title when Readability emits title-only text; it continues extraction and returns visible body content when no configured provider output is available.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw `web_fetch` tool execution with the real Readability extractor and a deterministic HTML fetch fixture. External network access was not required for this proof.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a Node proof script that calls the real Readability extractor, then calls `createWebFetchTool().execute` with `{ extractMode: 'markdown' }` against the same title-only fixture with provider fetching disabled.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

  ```text
  web_fetch title-only fallback proof
  fixture url: https://fixture.openclaw.test/title-only-template-raw-text
  readability.title: "Sample Page"
  readability.text: "Sample Page"
  readability.titleOnly: true
  web_fetch.status: 200
  web_fetch.contentType: text/html
  web_fetch.extractor: raw-html
  web_fetch.title.hasSamplePage: true
  web_fetch.text.hasBodyMarker: true
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the fixture forced title-only Readability output, and with no configured provider output available, the `web_fetch` result used `raw-html` with the expected body marker present.
- What was not tested: the exact original reporter page, because the issue did not include a concrete URL or full HTML fixture.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the live proof uses a deterministic fixture so the title-only condition and expected body marker are reproducible. The configured-provider order is covered by a focused regression test that asserts provider output wins before local raw HTML cleanup when a provider is configured.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the new focused regressions failed before this fix: the nested-template raw-text case surfaced fake script body text, and the provider-order guard caught the intermediate regression where local raw HTML cleanup ran before the configured provider.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts -t "ignores fake body tags in raw-text content inside inert templates"` — passed after the nested-template/raw-text scanner fix.
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts -t "tries provider fallback before body HTML when readability returns only the page title"` — passed after restoring provider-first order; configured provider output wins before local raw HTML cleanup.
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts` — passed, 62 tests.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added coverage for fake body tags inside raw-text content nested within inert templates, proving the scanner returns the real body marker and excludes the fake body text.
- Restored title-only/provider coverage so configured provider output is used before local raw HTML cleanup when a provider is configured.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The nested-template raw-text regression initially returned fake script body text instead of the real body marker.
- The provider-order guard caught an intermediate regression where recoverable local body HTML bypassed the configured provider.

If no test was added, why not?

- Not applicable; focused regression coverage was added/updated for the scanner gap and provider compatibility boundary.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. For title-only Readability pages, `web_fetch` now continues extraction instead of stopping at the title. Configured provider order is preserved, and local raw HTML body cleanup is used only when provider output is unavailable.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No expansion of capability. The change stays inside `web_fetch` extraction and does not broaden network access, auth, secrets, or tool execution.

What is the highest-risk area?

Compatibility for title-only pages: `web_fetch` now treats title-only Readability as incomplete and may proceed to configured provider routing or local raw HTML cleanup instead of returning only the title.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The canonical source of truth remains `web_fetch` runtime extraction: Readability first, configured provider routing second, local raw HTML cleanup last for HTML extraction failures.
- The change is limited to title-only Readability output; normal successful Readability output is unchanged.
- The focused provider regression asserts that configured provider output still wins before local raw HTML cleanup.
- Raw HTML cleanup stays runtime-local to `web_fetch`; shared conversion helpers and public API shape are unchanged.
- Related open PR scan result: PR #89028 is the active repair path for #82685, and current main did not already contain this title-only/body-boundary fix when this update started.
- Patch-quality note: the added `null`/`undefined` parser results are explicit scanner miss signals for malformed/non-tag input, not symptom masking; they allow scanning to continue until the real body boundary is found or no body is present.
- Size note: the diff is confined to one runtime file and its focused tests; the line count is mostly regression coverage for existing review findings in this PR.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after the updated branch is submitted and CI settles.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Maintainer: review the title-only extraction behavior with provider-first compatibility preserved.
- CI: rerun on the updated PR branch after submission.
- External proof: no exact reporter URL/HTML was available, so proof uses representative deterministic HTML that reproduces the title-only/body-cleanup invariant.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001/RF-008 fixed: raw-text content inside inert/nested templates is skipped while scanning body boundaries.
- RF-002/RF-004/RF-006 fixed: PR body now includes copied real `web_fetch` tool output for the title-only/body-cleanup behavior and describes the linked issue limitation.
- RF-003/RF-005 fixed: provider-first order is preserved for configured providers, addressing ClawSweeper's regression finding about local raw HTML cleanup bypassing configured provider routing.
- RF-007 fixed: author-side proof and regression-test gaps are addressed in this update.

PR surface: Source +2. Total +2 across 1 file.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows `openclaw config validate` reaches `OpenClawSchema.safeParse`, while current `main` resolves command-bearing MCP servers as stdio even though the schema rejects explicit `transport: "stdio"`; I did not run the CLI because this review is read-only.

target.title: fix(config): add stdio to McpServerSchema transport union
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw config validate` rejects `transport: "stdio"` on command-bearing MCP servers, even though the runtime (`resolveMcpTransportConfig`) correctly resolves any server with a `command` field as stdio — creating an inconsistency between validator and runtime.

The fix adds `z.literal("stdio")` to the `transport` union in `McpServerSchema` (src/config/zod-schema.ts:395), so the schema matches what the runtime already accepts. The runtime's resolver already ignores the `transport` value when a `command` is present (it always returns `kind: "stdio"`), so this is purely a schema-side alignment.

**Change**: one line, one file.

Fixes #95082

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `config validate` now accepts `transport: "stdio"` alongside `command` on an MCP server definition, matching the runtime behavior instead of producing a false-positive validation error.

**Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 24.04 (Linux 6.8.0-generic), Node v24, pnpm 11.2.2, OpenClaw build from current main

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
openclaw mcp set demo-stdio '{"command":"npx","args":["-y","@modelcontextprotocol/server-memory"],"transport":"stdio"}'
openclaw config validate
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
$ node openclaw.mjs config validate
Config valid: ~/.openclaw-dev/openclaw.json
```

**Observed result after the fix**: `config validate` passes with `Config valid:` — no longer rejects the `"stdio"` transport value. The configured MCP server with `command` + `transport: "stdio"` is accepted by the validator.

**What was not tested**: Full runtime MCP connectivity (requires credentials/auth) — this is a config-schema-only change; the runtime resolver was already tested separately and unchanged.

## Tests and validation

### Unit tests

```
$ pnpm test -- src/agents/bundle-mcp-config.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
   Start at  05:14:19
   Duration  813ms
```

### Build

```
$ pnpm build
✓ built in 244.3s
[build-all] phase timings: total 244.3s
```

### Real output: config validate before vs after

**Before** (without this patch — on current main):
The `transport` union only accepts `"sse" | "streamable-http"`, so a config with `transport: "stdio"` fails `config validate` with a Zod union error.

**After** (with this patch):
```
$ node openclaw.mjs config validate
Config valid: ~/.openclaw-dev/openclaw.json
```

### Diff
```diff
-    transport: z.union([z.literal("sse"), z.literal("streamable-http")]).optional(),
+    transport: z.union([z.literal("stdio"), z.literal("sse"), z.literal("streamable-http")]).optional(),
```

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible — adds a new allowed value to an existing union without removing or changing any existing behavior.
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — `config validate` passes on the baseline dev config before and after.
- [ ] I have updated relevant documentation — the docs already steer users to omit `transport` for stdio servers; the schema now accepts the explicit value for robustness.
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary — no breaking changes.

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [ ] All CI checks passed

target.title: fix(agent): load shared tools bootstrap instructions
target.body:
## Summary
- Auto-load `TOOLS_SHARED.md` as an optional workspace bootstrap file.
- Route `TOOLS_SHARED.md` through Codex inherited developer instructions alongside `TOOLS.md`.
- Allow the intended workspace-local symlink shape: `<agent>/TOOLS_SHARED.md -> ../shared/TOOLS.md`, while rejecting other sibling targets.
- Document the public bootstrap surface, subagent/cron context behavior, bootstrap-extra-files behavior, and the exact sibling symlink exception.

## Compatibility and security-boundary intent

This PR intentionally adds a new optional workspace bootstrap file surface. Existing workspaces that already contain `TOOLS_SHARED.md`, or the exact supported sibling symlink `<agent-workspace>/TOOLS_SHARED.md -> ../shared/TOOLS.md`, will start injecting that file after upgrade.

The cross-workspace read exception is intentionally narrow: it only applies to `TOOLS_SHARED.md`, only accepts the sibling shared target `../shared/TOOLS.md` after realpath comparison, and continues to reject other sibling workspace targets. This preserves the workspace-root read boundary while supporting the documented multi-agent shared-tools layout.

`TOOLS_SHARED.md` follows the same lifetime class as `TOOLS.md`: native Codex receives both as inherited developer instructions, subagents receive `AGENTS.md`/`TOOLS.md`/`TOOLS_SHARED.md`, and cron sessions include `TOOLS_SHARED.md` alongside the existing cron bootstrap allowlist.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Workspace bootstrap now loads a workspace-local `TOOLS_SHARED.md` entry so an agent can combine its own `TOOLS.md` with shared tool guidance.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout with a redacted agent workspace using the intended `TOOLS_SHARED.md -> ../shared/TOOLS.md` symlink shape. No channel credentials or provider API keys were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local loader probe against the redacted workspace after applying this patch.
- Evidence after fix: Copied live command output with machine/user paths and file content redacted:

```json
{
  "workspaceDir": "<agent-workspace>",
  "link": "TOOLS_SHARED.md",
  "symlinkTarget": "../shared/TOOLS.md",
  "loaded": true,
  "loadedName": "TOOLS_SHARED.md",
  "loadedPath": "<agent-workspace>/TOOLS_SHARED.md",
  "contentPresent": true
}
```

- Observed result after fix: `loadWorkspaceBootstrapFiles()` returned `TOOLS_SHARED.md` as loaded from the workspace-local symlink path, with content present and without exposing the shared file content.
- What was not tested: No full live Codex model turn was sent; this proof covers the workspace bootstrap loader behavior changed by this PR.

## Validation

After rebasing onto current `origin/main`:

- `pnpm check:docs`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check`
- `pnpm vitest run src/agents/workspace.test.ts src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts test/scripts/prompt-snapshots.test.ts -- --runInBand` — 6 files / 268 tests passed
- `pnpm vitest run src/agents/workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts -- --runInBand` — 3 files / 16 tests passed
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-context.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts src/agents/workspace.ts src/agents/workspace.test.ts src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts src/agents/system-prompt.ts test/helpers/agents/happy-path-prompt-snapshots.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-context.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts src/agents/workspace.ts src/agents/workspace.test.ts src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts src/agents/system-prompt.ts test/helpers/agents/happy-path-prompt-snapshots.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Note: a local full `pnpm lint:core` run on the rebased branch reported three `no-unnecessary-type-assertion` findings in `src/commands/test-runtime-config-helpers.ts`, outside this PR's changed files. Changed-file oxlint for this PR's source/test files passed.
