target.title: fix(gateway): resolve plugin-registered gateway methods through live registry
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: `openclaw gateway call <plugin-method>` returns `INVALID_REQUEST: unknown method` for plugin gateway methods registered via `api.registerGatewayMethod()`, even though `plugins inspect --runtime` confirms the registration.
- **Root cause**: `handleGatewayRequest()` preferred `opts.methodRegistry`, a startup-time snapshot passed from the WS handler. Plugin methods registered after that snapshot (hot-load / late-init) lived only in the live active plugin registry, so dispatch never reached them.
- **Fix**: Always build the per-request registry via `createRequestGatewayMethodRegistry()`, which reads `getPluginRegistryState().activeRegistry`.
- **Regression test**: `src/gateway/server-methods.plugin-gateway-dispatch.test.ts` dispatches `demo.ping` while passing an empty stale `methodRegistry`, reproducing the startup-snapshot miss.

Closes #94127

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Plugin-registered gateway RPC methods must resolve through the live plugin registry even when callers pass a stale startup `methodRegistry` snapshot.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux WSL2, Node 22, source checkout on this branch (`8efcc991054`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods.plugin-gateway-dispatch.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/princeht/fire_project/openclaw


 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  2 passed (2)
   Start at  21:30:58
   Duration  1.23s (transform 429ms, setup 331ms, import 530ms, tests 108ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 14.07s
```

- **Observed result after fix**: Regression test passes: `handleGatewayRequest` invokes the live `demo.ping` handler and responds `{ ok: true, ts: 42 }` despite an empty stale `methodRegistry` argument mirroring the startup snapshot path.
- **What was not tested**: Live gateway end-to-end with real plugin install + `openclaw gateway call` on a running gateway process.

## Verification

- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods.plugin-gateway-dispatch.test.ts`

target.title: fix(slack): preserve custom identity on message edits
target.body:
fix(slack): preserve custom identity on message edits

## Summary

- **Problem:** Slack `chat.update` silently drops `username`/`icon_url`/`icon_emoji` parameters when editing messages. Custom agent display names and avatars revert to the bot's default after any edit (tool calls, streaming updates, partial responses).
- **Fix:** Thread `SlackSendIdentity` through `editSlackMessage` and all its callers, mirroring the same mutually-exclusive `icon_url`/`icon_emoji` branching logic from the send path.
- **Out of scope:** Direct `chat.update` callers in approval-handler, interactions, and assistant modules (these may not use custom identity). Discord and Telegram don't need this fix (identity is webhook-level or bot-level, not per-message).

## Linked context

Closes #58737

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Agent display name and avatar revert to bot default when Slack messages are edited via `chat.update`. Initial `chat.postMessage` correctly uses custom identity, but edits drop it.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 10, Node.js v22.22.3, OpenClaw latest main, local checkout with the patch applied.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. Ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/action-runtime.test.ts` — 63/63 passed
  2. Ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/draft-stream.test.ts` — 11/11 passed
  3. Verified `editSlackMessage` now accepts `identity` parameter and builds correct payload
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/action-runtime.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  63 passed (63)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/draft-stream.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  11 passed (11)
```
- **Observed result after fix:** `editSlackMessage` now accepts an optional `identity` parameter and includes `username`/`icon_url`/`icon_emoji` in the `chat.update` payload. Draft stream edits now pass `identity: params.identity` to preserve custom agent identity during streaming updates. Preview finalize and dispatch now forward `slackIdentity` to edit calls.
- **What was not tested:** Live Slack workspace with custom agent identity (requires Slack bot setup). The test verifies the function signature and payload construction, not actual Slack API behavior.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/action-runtime.test.ts` — 63/63 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/draft-stream.test.ts` — 11/11 passed
- No CHANGELOG.md edits

## Risk checklist

- userVisible: Yes (agent identity preserved on message edits)
- configEnvMigration: No
- securityAuthNetwork: No
- Mitigation: Identity payload is only included when explicitly provided. Follows same pattern as send path. Graceful degradation if `chat:write.customize` scope is missing.

## Current review state

- Ready for review

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Direct after-fix artifacts — mirror-render screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png), multi-account screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png), and the full **destination-revocation run + gateway log** [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The revocation log shows the mirror deliver when enabled (`messageId=0` synthetic inbound + `outbound send ok chatId=…`), then `[outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed … (destination disabled/revoked)` after the group is disabled. - **Observed result after fix**: (a) Enabled group: the pinned Telegram thread receives the webchat-triggered turn's real reply, rendered natively (verbose `💬`+`🛠️` per the destination's config), exactly once. (b) **Disabled (revoked) group**: zero sends to the group and the tdlib driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE` — neither the native mirror nor the echo fallback delivers. Confirmed on both hot-reload and gateway-restart disable, at current head `0193a3d`. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Stop/reload window** (commit `8519433`): a channel that unregisters its admission predicate (account stop/reload drops the predicate *and* the dispatcher together) must not revert to admit-all. A sticky, import-cycle-free `channel-mirror-capability` marker records channels that have registered a mirror dispatcher; the echo gate then **fails closed** for a mirror-capable channel whenever no predicate resolves, so the raw echo can't leak to a revoked destination during the unregister window. Non-mirror channels admit echoes unchanged. Unit-covered (`channel-admission.test.ts`). **Telegram DM denial** (commits `219810d`, `fbd5ee2`): the same gap existed for a pinned **DM**. Three parts: (a) `enforceTelegramDmAccess` denial returned null *without* the revocation marker → `dispatchMirror` threw → unmark → raw echo could reach the denied DM; now a denied DM in a mirror context calls `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked` (keep the mark, suppress the post-hoc). (b) The synthetic mirror inbound used sender id `0`, so the DM access gate *rejected every already-authorized (paired/allowlisted) DM*; for a private chat the chat id **is** the target user's id, so it's now the synthetic sender — an authorized DM mirrors, a revoked one is denied. (c) The echo-admission predicate now re-runs **live DM authorization** (`resolveTelegramDmAllow` + `isTelegramDmAccessAllowed`) for a direct chat, so the raw echo also honors DM policy/pairing/allowlist revocation (the predicate is async; admission errors fail closed). Unit-covered: `bot-message-context.mirror.test.ts` (DM-denial → `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked`), `channel-admission.test.ts` (async predicate + fail-closed), `echo.test.ts` (async suppression). **Live-proven** (real DM: driver/Cameron DMs bot #2, `/pin on` in the DM): with `dmPolicy: open` the authorized DM mirrors (validating the sender-identity fix); after `dmPolicy: disabled` the DM gets **zero** sends, the driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`, and the gateway logs `Echo delivery suppressed … telegram:8661849123 (destination disabled/revoked)` — see the *DIRECT-DM REVOCATION* section of [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). **Registry ownership** (open maintainer decision): the mirror-dispatcher + echo-admission registries are global, caller-keyed, last-wins maps with no per-plugin ownership enforcement. They are exported from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound` and **only the bundled telegram channel uses them**. Whether to keep them as a public seam, hand them out through an owner-scoped registrar, or keep them internal is a maintainer API call — flagged explicitly in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`. (An attempt to hide them on a repo-local subpath was reverted: it fought the extension package-boundary + surface-budget guards for no real gain.) The branch is also **merged up to current `openclaw/openclaw` main** (conflicts in `send.ts` / `agent-runner-execution.ts` resolved). The plugin-SDK surface-budget guard is kept and passing; its budgets were raised to the merged-main surface actuals (the +10 entrypoints / +333 exports growth is from main's 203 commits, not this PR's ~6 exports). **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a gateway scheduled reaper for terminal sessions with `pendingFinalDelivery`, exports channel resolution, and adds focused reaper and CI-shard coverage.

PR surface: Source +307, Tests +158. Total +465 across 6 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level. The linked report provides a concrete stuck `pendingFinalDelivery` state, current main has no steady-state reaper, and the current writer does not seed a stable pending-final intent id.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] The remaining blocker is an owner decision about durable-delivery idempotency and session-marker reconciliation, not a safe autonomous repair on this PR branch.

target.title: fix(gateway): deliver replies stranded in pendingFinalDelivery (#93625)
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a steady-state reaper that delivers a reply stranded in `pendingFinalDelivery` once its owning run is definitively terminal, instead of leaving it until the next inbound dispatch or a manual gateway restart (#93625).
- Gate the flush on the persisted terminal `status` plus the absence of a live embedded run — not the in-memory reply-run/lane flag, which a phantom owner left by a no-op recovery keeps set.
- Keep the flush restart-idempotent: deliver keyed by `pendingFinalDeliveryIntentId`, then clear `pendingFinalDelivery` atomically.

## Real behavior proof

behavior: A reply written to `pendingFinalDelivery` by a completed embedded run whose terminal delivery was busy-skipped on a wedged lane should be delivered once the owning run is definitively terminal — exactly once, without re-running the agent — instead of remaining stranded until an operator restarts the gateway.

environment: Local source checkout rebased on `f3f2d398f6`, Node `v22.22.2`, branch `fix/reap-stranded-pending-final-delivery`.

steps:

1. Traced current `main`: stuck-session recovery no-ops against the wedged state — `abortAndDrainEmbeddedAgentRun` only force-clears when abort/drain fails (`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.ts:705`), and `resetCommandLane` is skipped (`released=0`) when a fresh run already took the lane (`src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.ts`); `pendingFinalDelivery` then only replays on the next inbound or a restart.
2. Added the pure reaper pass (`src/infra/pending-final-delivery-reaper.ts`) and a gateway scheduled-service host (`src/gateway/pending-final-delivery-reaper-service.ts`) that resolves the route, delivers through `sendDurableMessageBatch`, and clears the entry atomically. Started from `activateGatewayScheduledServices` (unref'd interval, skipped under vitest).
3. Added unit coverage for both invariants: no flush while a run is live or status is non-terminal; flush on a terminal+stranded entry; a cleared entry is not re-delivered on a later pass; a failed send records an attempt without clearing; plus grace window and attempt cap.
4. Ran the focused reaper test, the gateway runtime-services + CI test-plan tests, the core type gate, the deprecated-API guard, lint, and format.

evidence:

```text
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  7 passed (7)        # pending-final-delivery-reaper.test.ts
 Tests  16 passed (16)           # ci-node-test-plan.test.ts
 deprecated API usage guard passed
```

observedResult: The reaper delivers a definitively-terminal stranded reply once and clears `pendingFinalDelivery`; it does not fire while the owning run is still live/non-terminal, within the grace window, or past the attempt cap; and a cleared entry is never re-delivered.

notTested: A live gateway end-to-end run delivering a stranded reply to a real channel (no channel environment available here). The send path outside the normal dispatch flow + idempotency are the dispatcher edge case flagged for review. Broad CI gates (import-cycle / architecture / full suite) run in cloud CI.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/pending-final-delivery-reaper.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts test/scripts/ci-node-test-plan.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json`
- `node scripts/check-deprecated-api-usage.mjs`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint <changed files>` and `oxfmt --check <changed files>`

Related: #93625

target.title: fix(status): bound systemd service probes so status cannot hang on a wedged systemctl (#84698)
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes `openclaw status` hanging until an external timeout (exit 124) when the systemd user/system manager is wedged: the service-summary probe spawned `systemctl` with no deadline, so `--timeout` was advertised but never applied to it.
- Threads the status read deadline from `resolveStatusServiceSummaries` → `readGatewayServiceState` → the systemd read helpers → `execSystemctl`/`execSystemctlUser`, and passes it to `execFile` as `timeout` + `killSignal: SIGKILL` so a stuck `systemctl` child is killed and the command fails soft to a degraded ("unknown") service row instead of hanging. A default 5s bound applies when `status` runs without `--timeout`.
- Leaves control/install paths (`systemctl start/stop/enable/...`) unchanged — they pass no timeout, so their behavior is identical to today.

## Linked context

Closes #84698

## Real behavior proof
<!-- before(bug真实存在) -> premise(成因为真) -> after(修后好使) -->

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw status` (and `status --all`) hangs indefinitely / exits 124 when `systemctl` does not return, ignoring `--timeout`.
- Real environment tested: Linux (WSL2, Ubuntu kernel), real `systemctl` replaced on `PATH` by a wedged double (`#!/usr/bin/env bash` … `sleep 120`) that simulates a stuck systemd socket. Production code path exercised: `resolveStatusServiceSummaries` → `readGatewayServiceState` → `readSystemdServiceRuntime`/`isSystemdServiceEnabled` → `execSystemctl`. This spawns a real child process (not a mock).
- Root cause / premise evidence: on current `main`, `resolveStatusRuntimeDetails` awaits `resolveStatusServiceSummaries()` with no timeout argument, and `execSystemctl` calls `execFileUtf8("systemctl", …)` with no `timeout`, so a non-returning `systemctl` blocks the event loop forever. `node:child_process` `execFile`'s `timeout` option kills the child on expiry (verified directly: callback fired at 1506ms with `killed=true signal=SIGKILL`), which lets the command settle.
- Before evidence (bug reproduced on main): driving the production `resolveStatusServiceSummaries(2000)` against the wedged `systemctl` double on pristine `upstream/main`, with an 8s watchdog:

```
{ "kind": "HUNG_8s", "elapsedMs": 8003, "rows": null }
systemctl invocations seen by the double:
  21:04:07 systemctl --user status
  21:04:07 systemctl --user status   (gateway + node probes — neither ever returns)
```

- Exact steps or command run after this patch: drove the production `resolveStatusServiceSummaries(2000)` on this branch against the same wedged `systemctl` double on `PATH`, capturing the returned rows and the double's invocation log.
- Evidence after fix: the probe now returns degraded rows well within the bound, and the double's `status` calls are killed at the 2s deadline before the `show` calls run:

```
{
  "kind": "RETURNED",
  "elapsedMs": 4313,
  "rows": ["systemd user: disabled", "systemd user: disabled"]
}
systemctl invocations seen by the double:
  21:03:33 systemctl --user status   (killed at 2s)
  21:03:35 systemctl --user show openclaw-gateway.service ...
  21:03:35 systemctl --user show openclaw-node.service ...
```

- Observed result after fix: `openclaw status` no longer hangs on a wedged `systemctl`; it returns a degraded service row and respects the configured deadline (worst case ≈ 2× the deadline per service for the sequential `status`+`show` probes, parallelized across gateway/node).
- What was not tested: the full `openclaw status` CLI end-to-end on this machine — on my checkout the command stalls earlier, before the service-summary stage (no gateway running), on clean `main` too, so the box cannot exercise the full CLI hang. The defect and fix were therefore proven at the owning component with a real wedged `systemctl` subprocess. launchd (macOS) and Windows scheduled-task probes are left unchanged here; the new `timeoutMs`/`GatewayServiceReadOptions` plumbing makes wiring them later trivial, and those are tracked by separate open reports.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the wedged `systemctl` is a deliberately hanging real binary on `PATH`, not the production systemd daemon; this matches the reported failure mode (systemd socket wedged before reply).

## Tests and validation

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/systemd.test.ts` (92 passed, incl. 2 new regressions)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/status-runtime-shared.test.ts src/commands/status.daemon.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` (clean); `oxfmt --check` and oxlint clean on the changed files.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes` — `openclaw status` now returns a degraded service row under a wedged service manager instead of hanging; happy-path output is unchanged.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

Highest-risk area: the shared `readGatewayServiceState` / `GatewayService.readRuntime` signature, used by gateway and node services.

How is that risk mitigated? The new `timeoutMs`/`opts` are optional; only status read paths pass them, so control/install flows and other callers keep their current (unbounded) behavior, and typecheck confirms all call sites.

Current review state: maintainer review and CI.

AI-assisted (Claude). Proof above was run manually.

target.title: fix(codex): backoff startup connection-close retries and surface exhaustion (#83959) [AI-assisted]
target.body:
> [AI-assisted] This PR was generated using Claude Code.

## Summary

- **Problem:** When the Codex app-server connection closes during startup, `startCodexAttemptThread` cleared the shared client and retried **immediately** — before the replacement app-server process had bound/become ready — under a tight 3-attempt budget. All retries landed in the same failing window and exhausted, so a scheduled/background turn failed even though the Codex route became viable moments later. Cron automation reported job failure with no task-level problem. (Issue #83959)
- **Why now:** P1 for background/cron Codex turns; the issue's own local mitigation confirmed the direction (raise the startup-close retry budget + add a bounded backoff).
- **Intended outcome:** Startup tolerates a short replacement-server warm-up window before declaring failure, and when it does give up it says so distinctly instead of surfacing a misleading "client is closed".
- **Out of scope:** The separate per-run replay retry gate in `embedded-agent-runner/run.ts` (already correct, untouched) and the shared-client eviction work tracked under #79495.
- **Success:** A transient startup connection-close no longer fails the turn when the server recovers within the bounded window; persistent closes fail with a distinguishable error.
- **Reviewer focus:** The retry-loop change in `attempt-startup.ts` (budget 3→5, added `computeBackoff`+`sleepWithAbort` backoff, new `CodexAppServerStartupExhaustedError`) and the two new regression tests.

## Linked context

Closes #83959

Related #79495 (shared Codex app-server client eviction across agents — broader, separate).

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No — external contributor fix for an open P1.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex app-server startup connection-close retry loop exhausted before the replacement server was ready (#83959).
- Real environment tested: Windows 11, Node v22.22.3, OpenClaw checkout at upstream/main (1c0b38f960).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-startup.test.ts -t "83959"` and `node .tmp/capture-83959.mjs` (a node script that drives the real `computeBackoff` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/runtime-env` through the startup-close retry model with "closes on first 4 attempts, recovers on 5th").
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):
```
$ node .tmp/capture-83959.mjs
BEFORE fix (budget=3, no backoff): {"outcome":"exhausted","attempts":3,"gaps":[0,0],"totalDelayMs":0}
AFTER fix (budget=5, bounded backoff): {"outcome":"recovered","attempts":5,"gaps":[542,1133,2115,4000],"totalDelayMs":7790}
Repro: closes on first 4 attempts, recovers on 5th
```
- Observed result after fix: BEFORE — startup exhausted at 3 attempts with 0ms gaps (immediate retries re-entered the same failing window), turn failed. AFTER — startup recovered on the 5th attempt with bounded, abortable backoff gaps (542/1133/2115/4000 ms from the real `computeBackoff`), turn succeeded. The regression test `surfaces a distinct startup-exhausted error when connection-close persists (#83959)` confirms persistent closes throw `CodexAppServerStartupExhaustedError` (original error as `cause`) instead of the raw "codex app-server client is closed".
- What was not tested: A live `codex` app-server process on this Windows host (no local Codex harness). macOS/Linux runtime not exercised locally. The full `attempt-startup.test.ts` file has pre-existing Windows-only timing flakiness in unrelated `vi.waitFor(interval:1)` tests (they time out at 15000ms on Windows; they pass on Linux CI) — not introduced by this PR.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Repro method = node capture script + scoped vitest regression tests (no live Codex app-server). The capture script uses the real exported `computeBackoff`/`BackoffPolicy`, so the backoff numbers are the actual codebase values, not hardcoded.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-startup.test.ts -t "83959"   # on unpatched main
 Test Files  1 failed (1)
      Tests  2 failed | 8 skipped (10)
 x retries startup across transient connection-close failures with a backoff (#83959)
 x surfaces a distinct startup-exhausted error when connection-close persists (#83959)
AssertionError: expected 0 to be greater than or equal to 1           # budget=3 < 4 closes -> exhausted before recovery
AssertionError: expected 'codex app-server client is closed' not to be 'codex app-server client is closed'  # raw error rethrown, no distinct exhaustion error
```

## Tests and validation

- Commands run: `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` (changed files, clean); `pnpm tsgo:extensions` (clean); `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` (clean); `pnpm exec oxlint --type-aware` (changed files, clean); `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-startup.test.ts -t "83959"` (2 passed).
- Regression coverage added: Two new tests in `attempt-startup.test.ts` covering the previously-**untested** startup connection-close retry loop — (1) survives N transient closes with bounded backoff and recovers, asserting `sleepWithAbort` is awaited between attempts with bounded delays; (2) persistent closes produce the distinct `CodexAppServerStartupExhaustedError` with the original error as `cause`, and the loop is bounded (no infinite retry).
- Failed before this fix: Both new tests fail on unpatched main (budget=3 exhausts before the 4th-close recovery; raw "client is closed" rethrown with no distinct exhaustion error).
- Note: `pnpm check:host-env-policy:swift` skipped on Windows (no Swift).

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes` — a Codex startup that previously failed after 3 immediate retries now retries up to 5 times with a bounded backoff (worst case a few extra seconds before a transient-close recovery or a cleaner failure).

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No` — the change only affects the Codex app-server startup retry timing/error shape; no auth, secret, network, or tool-execution surface touched. `extensions/codex/src/**` is not a CODEOWNERS secops path.

What is the highest-risk area? Added latency on a Codex startup that closes repeatedly during warm-up (bounded to ~8s worst case across 5 attempts).

How is that risk mitigated? Backoff only fires after a connection-close failure (never on the success path); `sleepWithAbort` is abortable so a cancelled/aborted run fails fast instead of stalling; the budget is still bounded (5) so a permanently-broken server fails rather than retrying forever; the distinct exhaustion error preserves the original cause for diagnostics.

## Current review state

What is the next action? Awaiting CI (incl. Real behavior proof check) and maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? External-fork heavy CI shards require maintainer workflow approval to run.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? None yet (new PR).

target.title: fix(memory-core): index memory path in FTS text for filename queries (fixes #94102)
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #94102.
- `memory_search` FTS rows indexed only chunk body text, so queries for date-like filename stems (e.g. `2026-06-17-1649`) returned unrelated files with `textScore: 0` even when the target file was indexed and content search worked.
- Include the memory file path in the FTS `text` column at index time (`${entry.path}\n${chunk.text}`) so filename/date tokens participate in hybrid keyword ranking.
- Vector embeddings and chunk body text are unchanged.
- Keyword-search snippets and lexical density scoring strip the indexed path prefix back off, so excerpts show the chunk body (not the path line) and stay consistent with vector-search snippets; the FTS index keeps the path purely for matching.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Exact filename/date-token queries fail to rank the matching memory file first despite a clean index and working content search.
- Real environment tested: Linux (WSL2) source checkout, Node 22, FTS-only memory backend (`provider: none`, vector disabled).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Built the local checkout (`pnpm build`).
  2. Ran a redacted behavior probe that indexes two memory files and executes `memory_search` for `2026-06-17-1649` through the bundled memory-core runtime.
- Evidence after fix:
  Terminal transcript:
  ```text
  $ node behavior-probe-memory-filename.mjs
  {
    "top": [
      {
        "path": "memory/2026-06-17-1649.md",
        "textScore": 0.000003905989965114915,
        "score": 1
      }
    ]
  }
  ```

  Snippet-cleanliness guard (run via `node`), asserting the keyword hit returns the chunk body and not the `memory/...` path line:

  ```text
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts
  ✓ searchKeyword filename token ranking (issue #94102) > matches date-like filename stems when path is included in FTS indexed text
    expect(results[0].snippet).toBe("Token has been expired or revoked Google OAuth Testing mode")
    expect(results[0].snippet.startsWith("memory/")).toBe(false)
  Tests  23 passed (23)
  ```
- Observed result after fix: Top result path is `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md` with `textScore > 0`; nearby file `memory/2026-06-17-1701.md` is not returned for the filename stem query. The returned snippet for that hit is the chunk body (`Token has been expired or revoked Google OAuth Testing mode`), not the path-prefixed FTS text.
- What was not tested: Live `openclaw memory search` with Gemini embeddings against a production workspace (requires provider credentials and reindex).

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts` — 23 passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:extensions` — exit 0.
- Regression coverage: filename/date stem ranks first with `textScore > 0`, and the keyword snippet equals the chunk body (asserts it does not start with the `memory/` path prefix).

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? Yes
Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No
Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No
Highest-risk area:
Hybrid memory search keyword ranking for existing indexes
Mitigation:
Change applies on reindex/write paths only; no config surface. Existing indexes need reindex to pick up path tokens in FTS text.

## Current review state

Next action:
Await CI + ClawSweeper review
Waiting on:
Maintainer review
Bot comments addressed:
Updated Real behavior proof after initial mock-only-proof gate failure

target.title: fix: refresh subagent idle timeout on successful tool calls
target.body:
## Problem

When a subagent (`sessions_spawn` with `mode: "run"`) performs long-running tool calls (`web_fetch`, `read`, `exec`, etc.), the LLM stream is idle. If no LLM token arrives within 120 seconds, the session is terminated with:

```
LLM idle timeout (120s): no response from model
```

This is particularly impactful for research/analysis subagents that perform multi-step data collection before generating their final report. The subagent may have completed all its data gathering and be about to synthesize the output, but the 120s window expires and kills the session.

`agents.defaults.subagents.runTimeoutSeconds` (default 900s / 15min) only controls total wall-clock time, not idle time between LLM turns — so a subagent can be well within its total budget but still get killed because no LLM output happened during tool calls.

## Fix

A **tool-activity heartbeat** mechanism that keeps the session alive when tools are actively returning results:

1. **New module** `tool-activity-heartbeat.ts` — provides `onToolActivity()` / `notifyToolActivity()` modelled on the existing `llm-request-activity.ts` pattern
2. **`llm-idle-timeout.ts`** — `streamWithIdleTimeout` now subscribes to both LLM-request activity AND tool-activity via `armTimer()`, so either signal resets the idle watchdog
3. **`attempt.ts`** — calls `notifyToolActivity(runAbortController.signal)` after every completed tool call (both success and error branches)

The 30-second heartbeat window is intentionally shorter than the 120s default idle timeout, giving slow tool calls multiple chances to refresh before the watchdog fires.

## How it works

1. Tool call starts → LLM stream is idle
2. Tool completes → `notifyToolActivity(runAbortController.signal)` fires → idle timer resets (30s window)
3. Next tool call starts within 30s → timer stays alive
4. Agent returns to LLM → `next()` resets the timer via existing `onLlmRequestActivity`
5. If neither tool nor LLM makes progress for 120s → timeout (as expected)

No new config fields. No breaking changes. Just a smarter timeout origin.

## Related

- Issue #94124
- PR #49834 — model fallback cooldowns

target.title: Fix custom scheme origin allowlist matching
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes explicit origin allowlist matching for custom URL schemes such as `app://localhost`, used by ClawControl.
- Node’s `URL.origin` returns `"null"` for non-special schemes, so OpenClaw was comparing `"null"` instead of the configured origin, causing `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins = ["app://localhost"]` to fail.
- The intended outcome is that custom-scheme origins with a valid host can be allowlisted explicitly, without requiring wildcard `"*"`.
- Intentionally out of scope: changing broader origin policy, default trust behavior, wildcard behavior, or same-origin/private-network fallback logic.
- Success looks like `app://localhost` matching only when explicitly listed in `allowedOrigins`, while existing HTTP/HTTPS origin behavior remains unchanged.
- Reviewers should focus on the fallback construction for `url.origin === "null"` and whether the added test coverage captures the intended security boundary.

## Linked context

Closes #46520

Related #28172

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request. This was investigated after reproducing the ClawControl connection failure on a real OpenClaw gateway setup.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
  - ClawControl could not connect to the gateway via WSS when `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` was set to `["app://localhost"]`.
  - The gateway reported the origin as not allowed.
  - Using wildcard `["*"]` worked, but that is broader than necessary.

- Real environment tested:
  - OpenClaw built from source on a VPS.
  - Gateway running in Docker.
  - Windows ClawControl app connecting to the gateway via WSS.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Rebuilt OpenClaw base image from patched source.
  - Restarted the gateway container.
  - Configured the gateway with:

```sh
openclaw config set gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins '["app://localhost"]'
```

  - Restarted the gateway.
  - Connected from the Windows ClawControl app via WSS.

- Evidence after fix:
  - This PR author is using the Windows ClawControl app to communicate through the patched gateway after setting `allowedOrigins` to `["app://localhost"]`.
  - Unit test output from the patched checkout:

```text
Test Files 3 passed (3)
Tests 57 passed (57)
Start at 11:37:09
Duration 6.18s (transform 5.84s, setup 3.74s, import 510ms, tests 1.08s, environment 0ms)
```

- Observed result after fix:
  - ClawControl connects successfully via WSS with `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins = ["app://localhost"]`.
  - Wildcard `["*"]` is no longer required for this app-origin case.

- What was not tested:
  - Other desktop-shell schemes such as `tauri://localhost` or `electron://localhost` in a live app.
  - Browser extension origins in a live extension environment.
  - Full project test suite.

- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
  - The live behavior proof was validated on my own deployment, not a public reproduction environment.
  - Runtime logs/screenshots are not attached here to avoid exposing private gateway details.
  - The focused regression test covers the allowlist behavior directly.

- Before evidence:
  - Before this patch, the same setup showed the ClawControl error:
    `origin not allowed`

pnpm vitest run src/gateway/origin-check.test.ts

How is that risk mitigated?

- The change does not broaden trust by default.
- Custom-scheme origins are accepted only when they exactly match an explicit allowlist entry, unless existing wildcard behavior is already configured.
- Existing HTTP/HTTPS behavior continues to use `url.origin`.
- Missing, malformed, or literal `"null"` origins are still rejected.
- Same-origin, private-network, loopback, and host-header fallback logic is intentionally unchanged.

target.title: fix(anthropic): handle max_turns stop reason and surface retry-limit details to users
target.body:
## Summary

- Treat Anthropic `max_turns` as a limit-ended assistant turn, not as a natural model stop.
- Map `max_turns` to the existing normalized `length` stop reason in both Anthropic mapper paths, matching the established `max_tokens` truncation semantics.
- Keep `pause_turn` as ordinary `stop`, and keep the retry-limit user-facing diagnostics added by this PR.
- Intentionally does not add a new public stop reason, change retry/failover policy, or touch config, schema, credentials, routing, or non-Anthropic providers.

## Linked context

Closes #78145

## Root cause and scope

- Fix classification: Root-cause fix.
- Root cause: Anthropic can return `max_turns`, but both OpenClaw Anthropic raw-to-normalized stop-reason mappers grouped that provider limit with ordinary `stop` semantics. That made a turn-limit cutoff look like a natural assistant completion instead of a limit-ended assistant turn.
- Why this is root-cause fix: The Anthropic mapper boundary is the source-of-truth boundary where provider stop reasons become OpenClaw's normalized `StopReason`. Mapping `max_turns` to the existing `length` limit-ended reason fixes the invariant at the source for downstream runner completion/retry decisions, rather than masking one caller after the wrong `stop` value has already been emitted.
- Why it matters / User impact: Users and runner logic can distinguish an Anthropic turn-limit cutoff from a natural completion, so partial assistant output is not silently treated as a complete final answer.
- What did NOT change: This PR does not change retry-loop limits, failover policy, timeout thresholds, provider routing, configuration, schema, credentials, transport credentials, non-Anthropic providers, or the public `StopReason` union.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: The canonical contract surface stays the existing normalized `StopReason` union; `length` is reused because it already represents provider/output budget termination (`max_tokens`). `pause_turn` remains `stop`, and only Anthropic's raw `max_turns` normalization changes in the two Anthropic stream paths.
- Patch quality notes: The retry-limit lines in this PR only surface the already-computed retry/timeout message to the user and regression-test that diagnostic text; they do not change retry counts, timeout thresholds, failover decisions, or breaker policy. The public-contract risk is intentionally bounded by reusing `length` instead of adding a new public stop reason.

## Regression test plan

- Target test file: `src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts`; `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts`; `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/retry-limit.test.ts`.
- Scenario locked in: Anthropic emits an SSE `message_delta.delta.stop_reason` of `max_turns` after partial text and usage. OpenClaw returns `stopReason: "length"` while preserving the partial text and token usage in both Anthropic stream implementations.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The regression tests exercise the two mapper boundaries where the bad raw provider value was normalized; the retry-limit tests only lock the related user-visible diagnostics so this PR does not rely on caller-side fallback behavior.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Anthropic `max_turns` responses were being normalized as `stop`, which makes a turn-limit cutoff look like a natural model completion. The fix makes both Anthropic code paths report `length` while preserving partial text and usage.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node v24.16.0 and the repository package environment. The runtime proof directly invoked OpenClaw's production `streamAnthropic` function against an Anthropic-format SSE `Response`, so it exercised the real provider stream parser and stop-reason mapper without making a live API call.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  pnpm exec tsx --eval '<script imports streamAnthropic, feeds stop_reason=max_turns, prints normalized_stop_reason, and exits nonzero unless the result is length>'
  ```
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Console output from the runtime stream proof:
  ```text
  Anthropic max_turns runtime stream proof
  raw_stop_reason=max_turns
  normalized_stop_reason=length
  content=[{"type":"text","text":"Partial turn"}]
  usage={"input":4,"output":8,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"totalTokens":12,"cost":{"input":0,"output":0,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"total":0}}
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The production Anthropic provider stream path consumed a `message_delta` with `stop_reason: "max_turns"`, preserved the partial assistant text (`Partial turn`) and token usage, and returned `normalized_stop_reason=length` instead of `stop`.
- What was not tested: A live Anthropic API run that actually exhausts the upstream turn budget, because forcing `max_turns` from the hosted API on demand is not deterministic.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The issue-specific runtime proof replays an Anthropic-format SSE response through OpenClaw's real provider stream code instead of waiting for a hosted Anthropic API budget exhaustion event.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
  ```text
  FAIL  Anthropic provider > maps Anthropic max_turns as a length-limited stop reason
  AssertionError: expected 'stop' to be 'length'
  Expected: "length"
  Received: "stop"

  FAIL  anthropic transport stream > maps Anthropic max_turns as a length-limited stop reason
  AssertionError: expected 'stop' to be 'length'
  Expected: "length"
  Received: "stop"

  Test Files  3 failed (3)
       Tests  3 failed | 183 skipped (186)
  ```

## Tests and validation

Runtime proof command:
```bash
pnpm exec tsx --eval '<script imports streamAnthropic, feeds stop_reason=max_turns, and verifies normalized_stop_reason=length>'
```

Focused regression command:
```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts -t "maps Anthropic max_turns as a length-limited stop reason"
```

Related coverage command:
```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/retry-limit.test.ts
```

Regression coverage added:
- `src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts` now covers Anthropic `message_delta.delta.stop_reason: "max_turns"` in the provider path.
- `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts` now covers the same stop reason in the transport-stream path.
- Both tests assert the normalized stop reason is `length` and that partial content plus usage are preserved.

The new regression failed before the mapper change with `Received: "stop"`, then passed after mapping `max_turns` to `length` in both Anthropic mappers.

Validation results:
```text
Runtime stream proof: normalized_stop_reason=length
Focused regression: Test Files 3 passed (3), Tests 3 passed | 183 skipped (186)
Related suite: Test Files 4 passed (4), Tests 190 passed (190)
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`) Anthropic turn-limit cutoffs are no longer reported as ordinary natural stops.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- Stop-reason classification can affect runner completion/retry decisions for Anthropic sessions that hit a provider turn limit.

How is that risk mitigated?
- The fix reuses the existing normalized `length` reason already used for limit-ended output (`max_tokens`) instead of adding a new public API value.
- The regression tests cover both Anthropic mapper paths and assert content/usage preservation, so only the completion classification changes.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Ready for maintainer review after this update lands on the PR branch and remote checks rerun.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- No known author-side code or local validation action remains. A live hosted Anthropic `max_turns` exhaustion event was not forced because it is not deterministic on demand; the runtime proof uses a provider-shaped SSE response through OpenClaw's production stream code.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- RF-001: The earlier ClawSweeper review did not complete because of a Codex infrastructure/output issue, so it did not provide a patch-quality verdict. A fresh local ClawSweeper commit review passed for the current committed diff; maintainers may still request a hosted ClawSweeper rerun if they want a remote bot verdict.
- RF-002: The real behavior proof request is addressed with copied runtime terminal output showing `raw_stop_reason=max_turns` becoming `normalized_stop_reason=length`, plus RED/GREEN regression evidence for both Anthropic mapper paths.

target.title: feat(discord): send up to 10 file attachments per message (#24196)
target.body:
## Summary

Closes #24196.

The Discord API accepts up to 10 file attachments per message, but the integration only ever uploaded a single file. This adds an ordered multi-attachment path so a message can carry up to 10 files in one native Discord send, with any overflow split into follow-up messages (the issue's preferred option over "1 message per file").

What changed:

- **Agent send action** (`handle-action.ts`, `runtime.messaging.send.ts`): read an ordered `mediaUrls` array param and forward it; `message` is no longer required when `mediaUrls` is present.
- **Portable outbound payloads** (`outbound-payload.ts`): when a payload resolves multiple `mediaUrls`, batch them into one native send (`sendDiscordMediaBatchPayload`) instead of one message per URL. The existing video+caption split is preserved via `shouldPreserveDiscordVideoCaptionSplit`, mirroring the single-media behavior in `outbound-adapter.ts`.
- **Low-level send** (`send.outbound.ts`, `send.shared.ts`): `sendMessageDiscord` accepts `mediaUrls` (falling back to the legacy single `mediaUrl`), and `sendDiscordMedia` uploads in batches of `DISCORD_MAX_FILES_PER_MESSAGE = 10`, applying caption/components/embeds/filename to the first batch and aggregating every `platformMessageIds`.
- **Shared contract testkit** (`outbound-payload-testkit.ts`): adds an optional `multiMediaMode: "native-batch"` so Discord asserts the single batched send; other channels keep the sequence assertion unchanged.
- **Docs** (`docs/channels/discord.md`): document passing ordered attachments via `mediaUrls` and the 10-per-message + overflow-split behavior.

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/discord` — 211 tests pass, including new coverage for batched uploads, the 11-file overflow split (10 + 1 follow-up), action param forwarding, and the native-batch payload contract.
- Core + sibling channel contract suites pass (`src/channels/plugins/contracts/outbound-payload.contract.test.ts`, slack/whatsapp/zalo) — the new testkit param is optional and defaults to the existing sequence behavior, so no other channel changes behavior.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` / `pnpm tsgo:test:extensions` — clean for the touched surface.
- Re-ran the full Discord + contract suites after rebasing onto latest `main`; all green.

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Discord message sends now upload up to 10 ordered file attachments in one message via `mediaUrls`, splitting any beyond 10 into follow-up messages, instead of dropping all but one file.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux (WSL2) checkout, Node 22, Vitest extension + core contract suites and `tsgo` typecheck lanes.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test extensions/discord/src/send.sends-basic-channel-messages.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-payload.contract.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.test.ts src/channels/plugins/contracts/outbound-payload.contract.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: New test `splits media attachments beyond Discord's per-message upload limit` asserts the REST body of message 1 carries files `1.png`..`10.png` with the caption, and message 2 carries `11.png` with no `content`; `uploads ordered media attachments in one Discord message` asserts a single POST with both files; the native-batch contract asserts one `send` call with `mediaUrls` set and `mediaUrl` undefined.
- **Observed result after fix**: 211 Discord tests + core/sibling contract suites pass; `mediaUrls` of length ≤10 → one Discord message with all files, length >10 → batched messages of 10.
- **What was not tested**: No live Discord bot round-trip (no bot credentials available in this environment); behavior is proven through the REST-body assertions and contract suites, not a real channel send.

target.title: perf(infra): skip tsdown cleanup when CI=true [AI]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** tsdown-build.mjs runs 4 cleanup steps (pruneSourceCheckoutBundledPluginNodeModules, pruneUntrackedGeneratedSourceDeclarations, pruneStaleRuntimeSymlinks, cleanTsdownOutputRoots) before every build, recursively walking dist/ and extensions/. On Windows NTFS this takes ~6 minutes.
- **Why it matters:** Zero benefit on CI because git reset --hard already guarantees clean state, and tsdown already has --no-clean for incremental builds.
- **What changed:** Guard the 4 cleanup steps behind `if (process.env.CI !== "true")`. On CI, skip cleanup entirely and let tsdown --no-clean handle it incrementally.
- **What did NOT change:** Local dev behavior is preserved (cleanup still runs when CI is unset).

## Linked context

Closes #

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No

## Real behavior proof

### Behavior or issue addressed
tsdown-build.mjs cleanup runs ~6 minutes on Windows NTFS with no benefit in CI (git reset --hard already guarantees clean state, tsdown already has --no-clean)

### Real environment tested
Linux x64, Node v24

### Exact steps or command run after this patch
`CI=true timeout 20 node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs` — tsdown starts immediately without running prune/clean functions. `timeout 20 node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs` — cleanup runs, then tsdown starts.

### Evidence after fix
Both invocations produce identical tsdown output. With CI=true, no prune/clean log output appears (cleanup skipped). Without CI, cleanup runs first then tsdown starts.

### Observed result after fix
CI=true skips all 4 cleanup steps entirely. Local dev (CI unset) continues to run cleanup as before. No functional change to tsdown output.

### What was not tested
Windows NTFS environment (no Windows machine available). The CI runtime difference is expected to be visible on the Windows CI runner.

### Proof limitations or environment constraints
Tested on Linux where cleanup is fast (ext4). The ~6 minute speedup is theoretical based on known NTFS performance characteristics of recursive rm -rf on deep directory trees.

## Tests and validation

- `node -c scripts/tsdown-build.mjs` — syntax valid
- `CI=true timeout 20 node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs` — tsdown starts, no errors, cleanup skipped
- `timeout 20 node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs` — cleanup runs, then tsdown starts
- Verified the CI env var matches the convention used in .github/workflows/crabbox-hydrate.yml (`CI: "true"`)

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? No

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No

What is the highest-risk area? None — CI-only guard, local behavior unchanged.

How is that risk mitigated? env.CI check is only relevant in CI environments.

## Current review state

Next action: Review by maintainers.

target.title: fix(tailscale): pre-clean stale serve entries on gateway startup
target.body:
## Summary

On each gateway restart, `tailscale serve --bg --yes <port>` appends new entries without removing old ones. The `--service=<name>` path is especially affected because service registrations are append-only in the Tailscale control plane. After a few restarts, `tailscale serve status` shows duplicate entries causing `ERR_SSL_PROTOCOL_ERROR` or `ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED`.

This PR adds best-effort pre-cleanup in `startGatewayTailscaleExposure` that calls `disableTailscaleServe` before `enableTailscaleServe`, reusing the existing shutdown cleanup logic.

## Root Cause

Three structural defects in `src/gateway/server-tailscale.ts`:

1. **No startup pre-cleanup**: `startGatewayTailscaleExposure` unconditionally calls `enableTailscaleServe` without checking or cleaning existing routes. `tailscale serve --bg --yes` is append-only.
2. **`resetOnExit` defaults to `false`**: The only cleanup mechanism (shutdown `disableTailscaleServe`) is opt-in. Most users never enable it.
3. **serviceName path highest risk**: `--service=<name>` registrations are append-only in Tailscale's service registry, accumulating duplicates on every restart.

### Solution Comparison

| Approach | Mechanism | Reuse | Risk | Score |
|----------|-----------|-------|------|-------|
| **A: Orchestration-level cleanup** ✅ | Call `disableTailscaleServe` before `enableTailscaleServe` in `startGatewayTailscaleExposure` | High — reuses existing `disableTailscaleServe` | Very low | **8.6/10** |
| B: Low-level auto-reset | Auto-reset inside `enableTailscaleServe` | Medium — changes function semantics | High — affects all callers | 6.1/10 |
| C: Serve + Funnel parity | Same as A plus funnel pre-cleanup | Medium — speculative | Medium | 8.0/10 |

**Selected: Approach A** — correct abstraction level (orchestration layer does lifecycle management), minimal change (+13 lines), lowest regression risk, reuses existing code.

## Changes

| File | Change | Lines |
|------|--------|-------|
| `src/gateway/server-tailscale.ts` | Pre-cleanup try/catch before `enableTailscaleServe` | +13 |
| `src/gateway/server-tailscale.test.ts` | 5 new tests + 4 updated assertions | +84/-2 |

### Behavior

- **serve without serviceName**: `tailscale serve reset` → `tailscale serve <port>`
- **serve with serviceName**: `tailscale serve clear <name>` → `tailscale serve --service=<name> <port>`
- **preserveFunnel**: Unaffected (early return before pre-cleanup)
- **funnel mode**: Unaffected (separate code path)
- **Pre-cleanup failure**: Non-blocking (try-catch best-effort)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Duplicate Tailscale serve entries accumulate on gateway restart because `tailscale serve --bg --yes <port>` is append-only and no pre-cleanup runs before adding new entries.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x64 (worktree), Node 22, vitest v4.1.8.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-tailscale.test.ts
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  28 passed (28)
   Start at  18:42:48
   Duration  2.76s

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 19.66s

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-tailscale.test.ts \
    src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts \
    src/infra/tailscale.test.ts
 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  165 passed (165)
```

**Evidence after fix**: All 28 tests pass including 5 new tests that verify: (a) `disableTailscaleServe` is called before `enableTailscaleServe` in serve mode, (b) pre-cleanup failure does not block serve enable, (c) `serviceName` is passed through to pre-cleanup, (d) funnel mode does not trigger serve pre-cleanup, (e) `preserveFunnel` skip path does not trigger pre-cleanup. Broader suite of 165 tests across related files all pass with no regressions.

**Observed result after fix**: The `startGatewayTailscaleExposure` function now calls `disableTailscaleServe` (best-effort) before `enableTailscaleServe`, ensuring stale serve entries from prior runs are cleaned up before new ones are added. The `invocationCallOrder` test proves the correct ordering.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end `tailscale serve status` verification on a real Tailscale-connected machine was not performed — this requires a Tailscale account with a configured tailnet. The logic of the `tailscale serve reset` and `tailscale serve clear <name>` commands is upstream Tailscale CLI behavior, not OpenClaw logic.

## Risks

1. `serve reset` clears all serve routes when not using `serviceName` — consistent with existing `resetOnExit` behavior.
2. First-run reset on old Tailscale versions may fail — catch block prevents blocking.
3. Startup latency: `disableTailscaleServe` has a 15s timeout, but typically completes in <1s.

Fixes #93936

target.title: fix(tools): surface hidden-tool diagnostics via onHiddenDiagnostic callback
target.body:
## Summary

The tool planner (`buildToolPlan`) silently hid descriptors with malformed availability expressions (empty `allOf`/`anyOf` groups), because it had no mechanism to surface `unsupported-signal` diagnostics to callers. Tools were hidden with detailed diagnostics but no observable signal reached the developer.

## Root Cause

In `evaluateExpression` (`availability.ts`), empty `allOf`/`anyOf` arrays correctly produce `unsupported-signal` diagnostics, but `buildToolPlan` consumes them silently — it pushes descriptors into the `hidden[]` array with no callback, logger, or event emitter to notify callers.

Additionally, `evaluateExpression` calls `.flatMap()` / `.map()` on `allOf`/`anyOf` values without checking they are actually arrays — allowing a crash path if non-array values bypass the TS type system.

## Fix

This adds an optional `onHiddenDiagnostic` callback to `BuildToolPlanOptions`. The planner invokes it for each diagnostic that hides a tool, giving callers a channel to observe authoring errors without coupling the planner to a specific logging mechanism.

It also adds defensive `Array.isArray` guards in `evaluateExpression` before `.flatMap()` / `.map()` calls to prevent crashes on malformed non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` values.

- `src/tools/types.ts`: add `onHiddenDiagnostic` callback to `BuildToolPlanOptions`
- `src/tools/planner.ts`: invoke `onHiddenDiagnostic` for each diagnostic that hides a tool
- `src/tools/availability.ts`: add `Array.isArray` guards on `allOf`/`anyOf` before `.flatMap`/`.map`
- `src/tools/planner.test.ts`: verify callback behavior across 3 descriptors (visible, malformed, env-missing)
- `src/tools/availability.test.ts`: verify crash prevention for non-array `allOf` and `anyOf`

## Alternative approaches considered

| Approach | Verdict |
|----------|---------|
| `console.warn` in `evaluateToolAvailability` (PR #92368, closed) | ❌ Violates purity contract of the evaluator |
| Full `resolvePluginTools` integration + recursive validation + SDK exports (PR #92411, open) | ❌ Too broad (530 lines / 10 files) — mixes crash prevention, production wiring, and SDK surface into one change |
| Bounded callback + defensive checks (this PR) | ✅ Keeps evaluator pure, adds observable surface at the planner boundary, prevents crash, minimal scope (82 lines / 5 files) |

## Verification

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/tools/planner.test.ts src/tools/availability.test.ts

 ✓  unit  src/tools/availability.test.ts (11 tests) 171ms
 ✓  unit  src/tools/planner.test.ts (7 tests) 29ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  18 passed (18)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/tools/

 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  20 passed (20)
```

Standalone crash-prevention proof:

```console
$ node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "import { evaluateToolAvailability } from './src/tools/availability.js'; ..."
Empty anyOf diagnostic: [{"reason":"unsupported-signal","message":"Empty availability anyOf group"}]
Non-array allOf (no crash): [{"reason":"unsupported-signal","message":"Empty availability allOf group"}]
Non-array anyOf (no crash): [{"reason":"unsupported-signal","message":"Empty availability anyOf group"}]
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Empty `allOf`/`anyOf` arrays in tool availability descriptors produce `unsupported-signal` diagnostics that were silently consumed by `buildToolPlan`. The planner had no mechanism to surface authoring errors to callers, and `evaluateExpression` could crash on non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` values that bypassed the TS type system.

**Real environment tested:** Linux, Node.js v22.19.0, OpenClaw upstream/main (`0842cb71eb`), branch `fix/92361-tool-availability-hidden-diagnostic`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd /home/0668000615/10239986/github/openclaw-worktrees/fix-92361-tool-availability-hidden-diagnostic
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/tools/planner.test.ts src/tools/availability.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```console
 ✓  unit  src/tools/availability.test.ts (11 tests) 171ms
 ✓  unit  src/tools/planner.test.ts (7 tests) 29ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  18 passed (18)
```

The `onHiddenDiagnostic` callback test verifies:
- Visible tools do NOT trigger any callback
- Hidden tools with `unsupported-signal` diagnostics trigger one callback per diagnostic
- Hidden tools with `env-missing` diagnostics trigger one callback per diagnostic
- Multiple hidden tools each produce their own callbacks

Crash-prevention proof:

```console
$ node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { evaluateToolAvailability } from './src/tools/availability.js';
const d = { name:'t',description:'t',inputSchema:{type:'object'},owner:{kind:'core'},executor:{kind:'core',executorId:'t'},availability:{allOf:'not-array'}} as any;
const r = evaluateToolAvailability({ descriptor: d });
console.log(JSON.stringify(r));
"
[{"reason":"unsupported-signal","message":"Empty availability allOf group"}]
```

No crash — returns a diagnostic instead of throwing `.flatMap is not a function`.

**Observed result after fix:** `buildToolPlan` now calls `onHiddenDiagnostic` for each diagnostic that hides a tool, giving callers a channel to observe authoring errors. `evaluateExpression` returns `unsupported-signal` diagnostics for non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` values instead of crashing with `.flatMap is not a function`. All 18 direct tests and 20 total tools-directory tests pass.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway startup with a production caller wiring the `onHiddenDiagnostic` callback, because `buildToolPlan` has no production callers on current `main`. The callback contract is verified through unit tests; production wiring is a separate integration task.

## What this does NOT change

- `evaluateToolAvailability` and `evaluateExpression` remain pure and side-effect free
- The `ToolPlan.hidden` array still contains diagnostics (backward compatible)
- This PR does NOT wire `buildToolPlan` into `resolvePluginTools` — production integration is a separate concern

Closes #92361.
